segments.s160-g57: نباشد شگفت ار شود ماه خوار is a negative-present/subjunctive clause meaning 'it would not be a wonder if...'. نباشد is a negative form functioning like 'there would not be' or 'it is not (a wonder)'; شگفت is the noun 'wonder' used predicatively; ار = 'if' introduces the subordinate conditional; شود is a present/subjunctive 'become' (third-person); ماه خوار is a noun phrase 'moon-despised' (i.e. 'the moon become despised/humbled'). The clause sets up a hypothetical cause for what follows.
segments.s160-g58: تو خورشید داری خود اندر کنار is the main clause: subject 'تو' (you) + object 'خورشید' (the sun) + present verb داری 'you have/possess'. خود is an emphatic/reflexive particle meaning 'yourself'/'indeed', and اندر کنار is a prepositional phrase 'at (your) side' or 'beside you'. Word order is standard Persian: topic/subject (تو) then object (خورشید) then verb (داری), with the locative phrase following.
segments.s160-g59: کسی کاو چو من دید بر تخت عاج is a relative clause introduced by کاو (an archaic fusion of 'که او' = 'who'), meaning 'someone who'. کاو functions here as the relative 'who' (not the literal verb 'to dig'). چو من 'like me' comes directly after the relative to mark comparison; دید is the past tense 'saw' (third-person); بر تخت عاج is a prepositional phrase 'upon an ivory throne' where بر = 'on/upon', تخت = 'throne', عاج = 'ivory'.
segments.s160-g60: ز یاقوت و پیروزه بر سرش تاج continues the description: ز here is the poetic 'of/from' meaning 'made of' or 'set with'; یاقوت و پیروزه list gemstones 'ruby and turquoise'; بر سرش 'on his head' (بر = on, سر = head, -ش = his); تاج 'crown' completes the noun phrase 'a crown of ruby and turquoise on his head.' The word order is noun phrase (materials) then locative then the head noun.
segments.s160-g61: نباشد شگفت ار به مه ننگرد repeats the conditional frame 'it would not be a wonder if...'. نباشد (as before) negates 'there would be', شگفت = 'a wonder'; ار = 'if'; به مه ننگرد is 'he does not look toward the moon' or 'does not regard the moon' where به = toward/at, مه = moon, ننگرد is the negative-subjunctive/present form of نگر 'to look/regard' (here used as 'not look/regard'). The clause gives a circumstantial action for the comment that follows.
segments.s160-g62: کسی را به خوبی به کس نشمرد is a clause with an object marked by را and a negated verb نشمرد 'would not reckon/consider'. کسی را 'anyone' (with را marking the object), به خوبی 'as good' or 'for goodness' (خوبی = goodness/virtue) and به کس here gives the comparison/recipient 'to anyone'. نشمرد (negative past/subjunctive form of شمردن) means 'would not count/consider'. Together: 'he would not count anyone among the good' or 'he would not consider anyone good.'
segments.s160-g63: اگر با من اکنون تو پیمان کنی is a protasis 'If ...' where اگر = 'if', با من = 'with me', اکنون = 'now', تو = emphatic 'you', پیمان = 'covenant/pledge', and کنی is the present/subjunctive of کردن 'to make/do' (second-person singular): 'if you now make a covenant with me.' Word order places temporal/agent elements before the verb as is common in Persian.
segments.s160-g64: نپیچی و اندیشه آسان کنی contains two coordinated imperatives/requests. نپیچی is negative imperative/subjunctive 'do not turn away/ do not shirk' (ن- prefix for negation + پیچی from پیچیدن 'to turn'). و = 'and'. اندیشه here means 'thought/intention' or 'worry' and آسان 'easy' (= 'at ease'); کنی is the present/subjunctive 'you make/do'. Together: 'do not turn aside, and make your mind easy' (i.e. 'don't refuse and ease your mind').
segments.s160-g17: فرود آمد is a compound past: فرود (descent/alighting) + آمد (come) = 'stepped down' or 'alighted.' The sequence و شد پیش اوی uses شد (past/preterite 'became/came to be') + پیش اوی (پیش = 'before/in the presence of' with the enclitic اوی = 'him') to mean 'came/stood before him.' Word order (verb then prepositional phrase) is typical of older/poetic Persian.
segments.s160-g18: به گوهر بیاراسته is a prepositional phrase modifying appearance: به here functions like 'with'/'in' (instrumental), گوهر = 'jewel(s),' and بیاراسته is a past participle describing being 'adorned' (poetic usage). روی و موی lists two body parts ('face and hair') in the normal noun coordination.
segments.s160-g19: سیاوش (proper name) is the subject; بر تخت زرین is a locative prepositional phrase ('on the golden throne') with زرین as an adjective after the noun (classical/poetic order). نشست is the past tense verb 'sat' (intransitive past).
segments.s160-g20: ز پیشش = 'from/in front of him' (ز = 'from', پیشش = 'his presence'). بکش کرده is a participial/perfect-like construction: بکش (root with b- prefix from کشیدن 'to pull/draw') + کرده ('having done') = 'having drawn/pulled.' سودابه دست places سودابه ('Sudabeh') before دست ('hand') in the typical Persian possession/order 'Sudabeh's hand' (the name is in apposition to the noun). Overall the clause means Sudabeh had drawn her hand away from his presence.
segments.s160-g21: بتان را is the definite direct object marked by را ('the idols'). به شاه نوآیین is the indirect object ('to/for the newly-appointed king'); نوآیین is an adjectival epithet 'new/novel' (here 'newly-installed' or 'recently appointed'). نمود is the past tense of نمودن ('to show, display') meaning 'showed/displayed.'
segments.s160-g22: که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'; بودند is the past plural 'they were.' چون is a comparative/simile ('like/as'), followed by گوهر نابسود = 'a jewel of no avail' (نابسود here means 'fruitless/to no avail'). The clause states a comparison: the idols were like jewels that were useless.
segments.s160-g23: بدو = 'to him' (dative/enclitic 'to/unto him'); گفت here is the reporting verb 'he said' (the provided senses list does not include the verbal sense, so treat it as the past of گفتن). بنگر is an imperative 'look!' and بدین is the poetic contraction equivalent to 'به این' ('at this' / 'to this') attaching to تخت و گاه ('throne and place') as the objects of the imperative.
segments.s160-g24: پرستنده functions as a noun/participle meaning 'worshippers' or 'those who worship.' چندین is an indefinite numeral 'several/many a.' بزرین is a poetic/archaic form meaning 'great ones' (great men/elders), and کلاه is 'hat' — together the phrase denotes numerous distinguished hat-wearers (i.e., many important men wearing hats) who worship the throne/place.
segments.s160-g9: The clause begins with the particle که introducing reported content ('that'); باید is a deontic modal ('must / ought to'). The second که repeats the subordinator and introduces the subjunctive clause رنجه کنی (رنجه + کنی), where رنجه is a noun/verb 'to be distressed / take trouble' and کنی is the 2nd‑person singular present/subjunctive 'you do' — together meaning 'that you take the trouble'. پای خویش is the direct object: پای 'foot' used idiomatically for 'coming/setting foot' and خویش + enclitic ش = 'your own', so 'set your own foot / come in person'.
segments.s160-g10: نمایی here is the 2nd‑person singular subjunctive/imperative sense 'show (me / display)'; the following مرا را is the object (مرا 'me' split as م + را, with را marking the definite object). سرو is a metaphorical noun 'cypress' used for a tall, graceful person; بالای خویش literally 'the height/upper part of your own' (بالای = 'height/top of', خویش + ش = 'your own'), so سرو بالای خویش = 'the cypress of your height' i.e. 'your cypress‑like stature'.
segments.s160-g11: بشد is an archaic, narrative/imperfective formula often translated 'so it came to pass' or 'he went/approached'; here it introduces the action. The sequence 'بشد هیربد با سیاووش گفت' places the light verb sequence around the proper name: هیربد is the subject 'the hirbad (chief priest)', با سیاووش is a prepositional complement 'with Siyâvash', and گفت is the main past action 'spoke' (narrative past). The word order (verb + subject + adjunct + verb) reflects poetic inversion common in epic.
segments.s160-g12: برآورد is a past tense / perfective verb 'brought forth / produced / drew out' (here: 'drew out'). پوشیده راز is a nominal phrase 'the hidden secret' (پوشیده 'hidden/covered' qualifying راز 'secret'). از نهفت is a source phrase: از 'from/out of' and نهفت 'that which is hidden/concealed' — together 'out of concealment'. Grammatically, برآورد takes پوشیده راز از نهفت as its object and source phrase: 'he brought the hidden secret out of hiding.'
segments.s160-g13: خرامان is an adverb/adjectival adverb 'with a proud, stately gait' modifying the verb بیامد 'came' (past). The name سیاوش is the subject. The final element برش is an enclitic construction: here it functions as a directional/relational unit 'before him / to him' (poetic contraction of بر + ش), so the whole unit means 'Siyâvash came proudly before him.' Word order places the adverb first for vivid description.
segments.s160-g14: بدید is the simple past 'he saw' (sense 'saw'). آن نشَست is a nominalized clause 'that (he) sat down / that one sat' (آن = 'that/there'); here نشَست is a past or verbal noun 'sitting / sat'. The coordinating و links the following nouns سر 'head' and افسرش 'his helmet' (افسر + enclitic ش = 'his helmet'), so the phrase describes what he saw: '(he) saw him sitting, (and saw) his head and his helmet.'
segments.s160-g15: This prepositional cluster sets the scene: به پیشش 'in his presence / before him' (به + پیشش), بتان نوآیین 'newly fashioned idols' (بتان plural + modifier نوآیین 'newly made') is the topical noun phrase, and به پای is an expression 'at the feet (of)...' that connects to the following تو (your) in the next segment. The order is topical and descriptive: 'before him, newly made idols, at your feet...'.
segments.s160-g16: تو گفتی is the 2nd‑person past 'you said'. The quoted clause follows: بهشت است کاخ و سرای literally 'paradise is palace and house' — predicate (بهشت) is fronted for emphasis, with the copula است 'is' linking to the coordinated nouns کاخ و سرای 'palace and house'. In modern English idiom this is rendered as 'you said, "The palace and the house are paradise."'
segments.s160-g1: بدین داستان نیز is a prepositional/ adverbial phrase meaning 'with this tale' or 'thus by this story' (بدین = 'به + این' in classical usage). نیز = 'also/too' attaches to the whole phrase. شب برگذشت is a simple past clause: شب (subject) + برگذشت (past verb 'passed by'); the verb is classical/poetic and means 'passed' or 'elapsed'.
segments.s160-g2: سپهر is the subject 'the sky/heavens'. از بر کوه تیره is a compound locative phrase: از + بر + کوه تیره = 'from/off/away from above the dark mountain' (classical Persian uses از بر to mark 'from off'). بگشت is a past tense verb meaning 'returned/withdrew' (archaic form of برگشت).
segments.s160-g3: نشست is a past verb 'sat' (here 3rd‑person past). از بر تخت سودابه functions as a locative phrase 'on Sudabeh's throne' (classical 'از بر' often marks position on/at). شاد is a postpositive adjective describing the subject ('joyful').
segments.s160-g4: ز introduces material or composition in poetic register ('of/from' = 'made of'). یاقوت و زر افسری is a noun phrase 'a diadem of ruby and gold' (یاقوت and زر = materials, افسری = diadem/ornament). برنهاد is a past transitive verb literally 'placed on' (بـ + نهاد ≈ 'put on').
segments.s160-g5: همه دختران را is the definite object phrase 'all the maidens' with the object marker را. خواند is a 3rd‑person past verb 'called/summoned' (خواند here = 'summoned'). بر خویش is a prepositional/reflexive phrase 'to her (own)self' — note that خوی + ش together form خویش 'herself', split poetically into two parts.
segments.s160-g6: بیآراست in the manuscript context functions as a verb/adjective meaning 'adorned/dressed' (the form is poetically compressed; some lexica list بی‌آراست = 'unadorned' but here the context requires 'she adorned them'). و coordinates the clauses. بر تخت زرین نشاند is a transitive past clause: نشاند (she seated/placed them) + بر تخت زرین (on the golden throne). نشاند is an archaic/transitive past of 'to seat/place'.
segments.s160-g7: چنین گفت = 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = past 'said'); با + هیربد ماه‌روی marks the addressee 'to/with the moon‑faced hirbad' (classical با can indicate the person addressed). هیربد = chief priest/learned man; ماه‌روی is an epithet 'moon‑faced/beautiful‑faced'.
segments.s160-g8: کز introduces a subordinate clause meaning 'that/from which' or here simply 'that' introducing the reported command. ایدر = 'here'. برو is an imperative 'go' and بگوی is an imperative/subjunctive 'tell' (classical paired imperatives: 'go... and tell'). با سیاوش functions as 'to Siawush' (with/unto Siawush). Word order is typical classical: conjunction → place → imperative verbs.
segments.s160-g97: "برین باش" is an imperative instruction meaning roughly "go there / be there": بر + (این) yields a locative sense and باش is the imperative 'be'. The following clause "و با شاه ایران بگوی" uses و 'and' plus the prepositional phrase با شاه ایران 'with the King of Iran' and the verb بگوی (an imperative/subjunctive form meaning 'speak/say'), i.e. 'and speak with the King of Iran.' Word order places the prepositional phrase before the verb, typical in older/poetic Persian.
segments.s160-g98: نگه کن is an instruction 'look/observe' (نگه 'gaze/watch' + کن imperative 'do'). The particle که introduces the subordinate clause 'that/so that' meaning here 'see what...'. پاسخ چه یابی ازوی literally 'what answer you find from him': پاسخ 'answer' + چه 'what' + یابی (a present/subjunctive form 'you find/obtain') + ازوی 'from him.' The clause thus means 'see what answer you obtain from him.'
segments.s160-g99: بخواهم is a first‑person present/subjunctive form of خواستن used here with a prospective sense 'I will/shall ask/seek'. The subject من follows the verb (verb–subject order common in Persian). او را is the definite/direct object (را marks specificity). و connects this clause to the next, and پیمان کنم is a light‑verb clause (پیمان + کنَم 'I make a pledge') where کنم is the first‑person present/subjunctive of کردن.
segments.s160-g100: زبان را places 'tongue/speech' as a marked object (را). به نزدت is a prepositional phrase 'to/into your presence' where ت is the second‑person enclitic on نزد. گروگان کنم is another light‑verb construction (گروگان + کنَم) meaning 'I make (it) a hostage' — کنم is again first‑person present/subjunctive; the object (زبان را) precedes the verb as is normal in Persian.
segments.s160-g101: که introduces the subordinate clause; تا ... نگردد is the classic 'until' construction. نگردد is the negative present/subjunctive of گردیدن (here best understood as 'turn/come'), so 'تا او نگردد' = 'until he turns/comes'. به بالای من is a locative phrase literally 'to/at my high place' = 'to my presence/side', with the preposition به.
segments.s160-g102: نیاید is a negative modal/optative form meaning 'should not/shall not come' (here impersonal in effect). The beneficiary/goal phrase به دیگر کسی 'to another person' precedes the theme رای من 'my counsel/opinion' (possessor follows the noun). The whole clause says the speaker's counsel must not be offered to anyone else while the previous condition holds.
segments.s160-g103: و دیگر here works as 'and moreover/again'; که introduces the subordinate temporal clause 'when/that'. پرسیدی is the simple past 2nd‑person 'you asked'. از چهر من is a prepositional object 'about my face/appearance' with the possessor من following چهر.
segments.s160-g104: بیامیخت is an archaic/poetic preterite/imperfect form (equivalent to می‌آمیخت) meaning 'mingled/was mingled'. The sequence با جان تو مهر من places the instrumental phrase 'with your soul' (با جان تو) before the theme 'my love' (مهر من), so the literal order is 'mingled with your soul my love' → 'my love mingled with your soul.'
segments.s160-g81: چنین ('thus') is an adverb introducing reported thought. گفت here is the past-tense verb 'said' but the provided senses list does not include the verb sense; با دل ('with heart') is an idiom meaning 'in his heart' or 'to himself'. که is the complementizer 'that' introducing the content clause 'از کار دیو ...'.
segments.s160-g82: مرا دور داراد is object + verb: مرا is the first‑person object ('me') marked by را (here split as م + را in the word list); دور داراد literally 'keeps (me) far' — داراد is an archaic/poetic third‑person present form (optative/indicative sense 'may he keep' / 'keeps'). گیهان خدیو is a title in apposition 'ruler/khedive of the world' (word order: noun + title).
segments.s160-g83: نه ... کنم is the negated finite clause structure: نه (not) + subject (من) + prepositional phrase (با پدر) + object noun (بیوفایی) + verb کنم ('I do/make' used causatively/idiomatically). Here بیوفایی کردن = 'to commit unfaithfulness/betrayal' so the clause means 'I will not be unfaithful to my father.'
segments.s160-g84: The second parallel clause repeats the negation by ellipsis: نه + preposition با + proper name اهرمن (Ahriman/the devil) + noun آشنایی ('acquaintance/familiarity') + فعل کنم. آشنایی کردن with someone = 'to associate/become familiar with' them, so the meaning is 'nor will I associate with Ahriman.'
segments.s160-g85: وگر introduces a conditional 'if'. سرد گویم is 'if I say [it] coldly/aloofly' (گویم = 'I say', here a 1st‑person present/subjunctive form). بدین is a prepositional contraction (به + این) 'to this', so بدین شوخ چشم = 'to this saucy/impudent eye' (an epithet for the addressee's glance). Note that the provided sense for گویم is not the verbal sense, so it is marked below as missing.
segments.s160-g86: بجوشد (third‑person present/subjunctive imperfective) means 'will seethe/boil' (emotionally). دلش is 'his heart' (دل + enclitic ش = his). گرم گردد uses the light verb گردد 'become' so 'grow hot/fervent'; ز introduces cause 'from/with' خشم 'anger'. The two verbs are parallel results: 'his heart will seethe and become hot with anger.'
segments.s160-g87: یکی is an indefinite subject 'a certain one / someone'. جادوی سازد literally 'performs/works magic' — سازد is the third‑person present/subjunctive form of ساختن 'to make/do' used here to mean 'does/makes (magic)'. اندر نهان is an adverbial phrase 'in secret / hiddenly'.
segments.s160-g88: بدو ('to him' / 'unto him') is a dative/adverbial 'toward him'; بگرود is a third‑person verbal form (from گرویدن) meaning here 'turns to / joins / attaches to' — i.e., the ruler gives his allegiance or turns toward that person. شهریار جهان is the title 'the king (lord) of the world', the subject/topic of this verb.
segments.s160-g161: وگر is the conditional 'if' (classical/poetic). سر بپیچی is a 2nd-person verb phrase ('you turn your head/you turn away') where بپیچی is the present/subjunctive/optative form of پیچیدن used to indicate turning away; ز فرمان من is a prepositional phrase 'from my command' with من functioning as the possessor ('my'). Word order is inverted for emphasis (particle + verb phrase before the prepositional phrase).
segments.s160-g162: نیاید is a negative verb meaning 'does not/will not come' (3rd-person form used here in a poetic/optative sense). دلت is دل + the 2nd-person suffix ت 'your heart', and سوی پیمان من is a prepositional phrase 'toward my covenant/pledge' (من again as possessor). The verb appears before its logical subject (poetic inversion).
segments.s160-g163: کنم is the 1st-person verb 'I do / I will make' (subjunctive/optative/causative nuance). بر تو 'upon you' marks the person affected, and بر پادشاهی 'upon (your) kingship' is the domain of the action; تباه is a predicative adjective 'ruined' — together the clause means 'I will make your kingship ruined' (classical word order places the verb early and the result/adjective after the affected noun phrases).
segments.s160-g164: شود is 'becomes' (3rd-person present/subjunctive), and تیره 'dark/ominous' is the predicate that follows it. بر روی تو is a locative phrase 'upon your face', while چشم شاه is a genitive noun phrase 'the king's eye/gaze' (owner after the possessed noun in Persian). The whole reads 'the king's eye will darken upon your face' — i.e., the king will look upon you with a dark/ominous gaze.
segments.s160-g165: سیاوش is the speaker's name; بدو is 'to him' (به + او contracted in classical/poetic style) indicating the indirect object; گفت is the past-tense 'said' (here introducing direct speech). هرگز مباد is an optative negative formula 'may it never be' (مباد = 'let it not be').
segments.s160-g166: که introduces the content clause 'that...'; از بهر دل is a fixed expression 'for the sake of (a) heart' (از بهر = for/on account of); سر دهم من به باد literally 'I give my head to the wind' (سر دهم = 'I give my head / I offer my life' — دهم is 1st-person present/subjunctive of دادن, and من is the emphatic subject 'I'). به باد is literal 'to the wind' but here idiomatic for 'throw away' or 'sacrifice to the wind'.
segments.s160-g167: چنین 'thus/so' modifies the action; با پدر بی‌وفایی کنم literally 'I do unfaithfulness with my father' but in context (bound to the previous optative negative) it means 'that I should be unfaithful to my father'. کنم is 1st-person subjunctive/optative 'I would do / I should do' — the negative sense is carried from the earlier 'هرگز مباد'.
segments.s160-g168: ز مردی و دانش جدایی کنم means 'I separate from / abandon manliness and knowledge'; ز is the preposition 'from', مردی here is the abstract quality 'manliness' (not simply 'a man'), و links to دانش 'learning/knowledge', جدایی 'separation' and کنم is again 1st-person subjunctive/optative 'I would/should do' — together, in context, 'I will not abandon my manliness and learning.'
segments.s160-g33: برفتند is the past-tense, 3rd-person plural verb ‘they went/left’. هر یک (literally ‘each one’) modifies the implied agents, and سوی تخت خویش is a prepositional phrase ‘toward his own throne’ with سوی (toward) + تخت (throne) + خویش (own). Word order is typical older Persian: verb first, then the distributive phrase and the directional phrase.
segments.s160-g34: This nominal/chunk phrase lists two groups/roles: ژکان (an obscure/poetic group-name) and شمارنده (‘the counter/numberer’). بر بخت خویش is another prepositional phrase meaning literally ‘upon/to their lot/fate’ (بر + بخت + خویش). The conjunction و simply links the two nouns; the phrase as a whole indicates these persons went to their respective fortunes/places.
segments.s160-g35: چو is a subordinating conjunction meaning ‘when’. ایشان is the third‑person plural pronoun ‘they/them’, and برفتند is the past 3rd‑person plural ‘they went/left’. سودابه گفت is the main reporting clause: proper name + گفت (‘said’ in the simple past). The sequence places the time clause first (When they left) then the reported speech act.
segments.s160-g36: که introduces the content clause (‘that/what’) or direct question. چندین here means ‘many/quite a few’ (intensifier), چه is the interrogative ‘what’, داری is the 2nd‑person present of داشتن ‘you have/are holding’, and سخن در نهفت is a noun phrase ‘words/speech hidden’ (سخن ‘word/speech’ + در نهفت ‘kept/hidden inside’). Together: ‘what (many things) do you have hidden?’
segments.s160-g37: نگویی is a prohibitive/negative form (ن- + گویی) addressing the addressee: literally ‘do not say’ or ‘do not tell’. مرا (here split in the text as م + را) is the object ‘to me’ with را marking the direct object. تا introduces the subordinate clause of extent/time ‘until’, and مراد تو چیست is an embedded question ‘what is your desire/intention?’ with چی/ چیست forming the copular question. Together: ‘Do not tell me until (I know) what your intention is.’
segments.s160-g38: که here gives the reason/explanation ‘for’ or ‘because’. بر چهر تو is a prepositional phrase ‘on your face’ (بر + چهر + تو). فر is the technical epic term for royal/divine radiance or splendour, and چهر (repeated) again ‘appearance/face’; پریست is an attributive/predicative form indicating relation to ‘pari’ (a fairy) — the phrase says that a fairy‑like radiance/appearance rests upon your face. This clause explains why the speaker reacts as she does.
segments.s160-g39: هر آن کس که is the standard relative/distributive ‘whoever, anyone who’. از دور بیند is a present/subjunctive/epic present form meaning ‘sees from afar’ (از دور = from a distance; بیند = sees). ترا (tokenized here as ت + را) is the object ‘you’ + object marker. The clause describes any person who glimpses the addressee from a distance.
segments.s160-g40: شود is the present/future/epic auxiliary ‘becomes’ or ‘will become’. بیهش is an older/obscure form (here serving an idiomatic role — roughly ‘enchanted/enamoured for him’ in context) and is marked in the lexicon as an irregular/obscure form. و connects the two predicates, برگزیند is the (present/subjunctive/epic) verb ‘chooses/selects’, and ترا is again the object ‘you’. The clause says that such a viewer becomes captivated and picks/chooses you.
segments.s160-g153: همی here is a continuous/emphatic particle ('still/continually') placed before the clause; روز روشن is a noun + adjective 'bright day' with the adjective after the noun (typical Persian order). نبینم is a negative present/subjunctive-form of دیدن used here as a first-person assertion 'I do not see' (classical style). ز introduces the cause 'because of', and درد names the suffering ('sorrow/pain') that is the reason for not seeing the bright day.
segments.s160-g154: برآنم is a fixed classical phrase meaning 'I am resolved / I hold that' (first person present state). که is the complementizer 'that' introducing the subordinate clause. خورشید شد is a simple past/resultative 'the sun became' (شد = 'became'). لاجورد is a predicate noun describing the resulting colour/state ('deep blue' / lapis lazuli).
segments.s160-g155: کنون 'now' opens the temporal statement. هفت سال‌ست is a verbless predication 'it is seven years' (سال‌ست = 'it is years'), and تا here functions in a classical sense like 'since' or 'for the period of' linking that duration to مهر من ('my love/affection') as the timespan's topic.
segments.s160-g156: همی again gives a continuous/emphatic sense 'continually' or 'still'; خون چکاند is a causative/perfective phrasing meaning 'has caused blood to drip' (چکاند is perfect/past meaning 'made drip' or 'dripped'). بدین is the prepositional combination ب- + دین (here functioning as 'to/onto this' = 'upon this'), marking the target, and چهر من 'my face' is the affected noun phrase ('چهر' = face, modified by the possessive من).
segments.s160-g157: یکی here functions adverbially 'once' / 'one time' (classical use). شاد کن is an imperative 'make (me) happy' with کن as the verbal imperative. در نهانی is a prepositional phrase 'in secret/secretly' (در = in, نهانی = hidden/secret). مرا is the object 'me' (here written as م + را in the segment), with را the definite/object marker attached to the pronoun.
segments.s160-g158: ببخشای is an optative/imperative form meaning 'grant' or 'bestow' (here 'grant me' rather than 'forgive'). روز جوانی is a noun phrase 'the day(s) of youth' where روز = day/time and جوانی = youth/prime. مرا again is the object pronoun 'me' with the object marker را attached.
segments.s160-g159: فزون 'more' or 'greater' opens a comparative idea; زان (از + آن contracted) means 'than that' or 'from that', so فزون زان که introduces 'more than that which...'. که is the complementizer 'that'; دادت (دادت) is a past tense 'gave you' (داد + ت = 'gave to you'). جهاندار شاه is a compounded honorific 'world-holder/sovereign king' (both nouns in apposition).
segments.s160-g160: بیارایمت is a future/intentional verb form 'I will adorn/array you' (بیارای- + مت = 'I ... you'). یاره is an object meaning 'beloved/companion' that the speaker will provide; و links the list items; تاج is 'crown', and گاه here names a 'place/occasion' or 'seat'—in lists like this it names another honour/thing to be given. The verb governs the following coordinated noun phrases as direct objects.
segments.s160-g129: ز هر چیز (prepositional phrase) = 'from every thing' with the preposition ز placed before هر چیز as in classical Persian. گنجی (indefinite) ‘a treasure’ follows, and بد آراسته is a descriptive participial phrase modifying گنجی; بد here is archaic/poetic in use and ambiguous in meaning (see senses). Word order is typical older Persian: preposition + noun phrase, then the (indefinite) noun + its adjectival participle.
segments.s160-g130: جهانی سراسر پر از خواسته is a nominal phrase: جهانی (’a world’/’worldly’) qualified by سراسر ‘throughout, entire’ and then پر از + noun meaning ‘full of …’. از here introduces the content (خواسته ‘desires/wishes’) and پر governs از. The whole phrase = 'a world entirely full of desires.'
segments.s160-g131: نگه کرد is an idiomatic verb phrase (literally 'made a gaze') meaning 'looked'; classical Persian often places the verb before the subject, so کرد is followed by the subject سودابه. خیره بماند combines the adjective/adverb خیره 'staring/astonished' with بماند (the subjunctive/present-form of ماندن used narratively) to mean 'she remained/was left staring.'
segments.s160-g132: به اندیشه is a prepositional phrase 'in/to thought' marking manner or mental state. افسون فراوان is a noun + adjective 'many/abundant charms/incantations.' بخواند is the third-person form of خواندن; here it functions narratively (subjunctive/formal present used as past) meaning 'she recited/uttered' (i.e. chanted the charms).
segments.s160-g133: که‌گر is an archaic fused conditional ('if'); the clause follows with subject او and the negative verb نیاید ('does not come' — here a subjunctive/negative form expressing hypothetical or potential). به فرمان من is a prepositional phrase 'at my command' that completes the condition.
segments.s160-g134: روا دارم is a fixed expression: روا 'permissible/just' with دارم 'I have' used as a verb meaning 'I consider/allow' (so 'I will consider it allowable'). ار (archaic = اگر) introduces the subordinate conditional 'if'; بگسلد is a subjunctive/optative form of گسستن 'to break' used here metaphorically 'take/rupture my life' = 'kill me.' جان من is the object 'my life.'
segments.s160-g135: This is a list: بد و نیک 'bad and good' joined with و هر چاره 'and every remedy/expedient'. کاندر is an archaic fused relative + preposition (که اندر) meaning 'that in' or 'which are in', linking the list to جهان 'the world' — i.e. 'every good and bad thing and every expedient that exists in the world.'
segments.s160-g136: کنند is the third-person plural of کردن 'to do/make' agreeing with the plural antecedent (all those things); in classical narration this present/subjunctive form serves as a general or past-habitual. آشکارا 'openly' and اندر نهان 'and in secret' are adverbial phrases making the contrast 'both openly and secretly.'
segments.s160-g25: This is a nominal phrase: همه (determiner) + adjective نارسیده + noun بتان + qualifier طراز. In classical Persian the adjective (نارسیده) precedes the noun (بتان) and همه quantifies the whole group. طراز here functions as a postpositive modifier meaning “fashioned/patterned” (i.e. ‘in the fashion of idols/beauties’).
segments.s160-g26: که introduces a relative clause modifying the foregoing phrase (“which/whose…”). بسرشتشان is built from ب- + سرشت + -شان and means “in their nature / in their constitution” (the b- is a classical prepositional prefix and -شان is the 3rd-person plural possessive). ایزد is the grammatical subject of the clause, and از + شرم و ناز expresses the material/cause (“out of/modulated by modesty and coquettishness”) — i.e. God made their nature from those qualities.
segments.s160-g27: کسی is an indefinite subject “someone.” کت appears here as a noun following کسی; in classical diction کت can mean “garment/coat” (though meter/usage may allow metaphorical senses). خوش آید is a light impersonal/present construction meaning “it pleases / is pleasing” (خوش + آید). ازیشان = از + ایشان “from/of them” indicates the source/target of pleasure. بگوی is an imperative/hortative form built on گفت/گو (“say/tell”) used here to introduce what should be said.
segments.s160-g28: This is a sequence of imperatives/commands. نگه کن is the colloquial/poetic imperative “keep (it), hold (the look)” (نگه = gaze/hold, کن = do!). بدیدار is the contracted/prepositional form (به دیدار) meaning “toward the sight/meeting” or “to the view”; here it marks direction or object of the gaze. و joins the clause to بالای اوی, where بالای = “upon/above” and اوی is an older/poetic form involving او + enclitic element meaning “him/his” — together “upon him / on his person.”
segments.s160-g29: چو is the temporal conjunction “when.” The subject Sیاوش begins the clause. چشم اندکی is the object/adjunct “a little (gaze/eye)” (اندکی following the noun gives the measure). برگماشت is a past/punctual verb here meaning “lifted/raised” (an older/poetic past form related to برداشت/برداشت — ‘took up / lifted’).
segments.s160-g30: ازیشان یکی = “one (of them).” ازیشان (از + ایشان) marks the partitive/source “from them,” and یکی is the numeral/indefinite “one.” چشم here means “gaze / look” and ازو (از + او) means “from him.” برنداشت is the negative past perfective (here negated by preceding context in the full line) meaning “did not take away / did not remove” — idiomatically “did not avert (her) gaze from him.”
segments.s160-g31: همه یک به دیگر is a common classical phrase “all, one to another” (i.e. they said amongst themselves). به is the preposition “to,” and دیگر here means “one another/each other.” بگفتند is the past plural “they said.” ماه begins the reported speech and is used metaphorically as the moon (a conventional image for beauty).
segments.s160-g32: This clause is reported speech compressed in classical style. نیارد is an archaic/poetic negative potential meaning roughly “would not dare / would not bring itself” (here: the moon would not dare). بدین is the fused prepositional phrase (به + این) meaning “to/at this”; in classical spelling the fusion appears as بدین. شاه is the indirect object “king,” کردن is the light verb “to do/make” used here with نگاه (gaze) to form the verbal idea “to cast/look” (شاه کردنِ نگاه = “to direct a gaze at the king”). نگاه is the noun “gaze/ glance” that completes the expression.
segments.s160-g73: من اینک به پیش تو استاده‌ام = subject + temporal adverb + prepositional phrase + predicate. من (I) is the subject; اینک is a temporal adverb 'now'. به پیش تو is a classical prepositional phrase 'before/in the presence of you' (به + پیش + تو). استاده‌ام is the perfect/present-state form of ایستادن (here written استاده‌ام), first person singular = 'I stand / I have stood', formed by the participle + the enclitic auxiliary -ام.
segments.s160-g74: تن و جان شیرین ترا داده‌ام = coordinated direct object + adjective + clitic object + verb. تن و جان 'body and life/soul' is a fixed paired object; شیرین qualifies them as 'sweet' (an epithet). ترا is the enclitic object pronoun ت + را (to/onto you / 'you' as indirect object in older poetry). داده‌ام is the perfect first person singular of دادن 'I have given'.
segments.s160-g75: ز من هرچ خواهی همه کام تو = prepositional phrase + relative phrase + noun phrase. ز من 'from me' (poetic surface order). هرچ خواهی is a relative clause 'whatever you wish/ask' where خواهی is second person present/future 'you want/wish'. همه کام تو = 'all your desires' (همه modifying کام, followed by the genitive/possessive pronoun تو).
segments.s160-g76: برآرم نپیچم سر از دام تو = two verb clauses giving promises/negations. برآرم is a first‑person singular present/subjunctive/future form (بر + آرم) meaning 'I will bring about / I shall fulfill' (verb + -م). نپیچم is negation (ن-) + first person present/subjunctive of پیچیدن 'to turn/twist' = 'I will not turn'; here it has an adversative sense. سر is the direct object 'head', از دام تو 'from your snare' shows the source/thing avoided. The clauses are coordinated: 'I shall fulfill ...; I will not pull my head out of your snare.'
segments.s160-g77: سرش تنگ بگرفت و یک پوشه چاک = idiomatic past action + coordination. سرش 'his head' (سر + bound possessive ش = his); تنگ بگرفت is a past-tense idiom literally 'took tight' (ب‑گرفت = took/seized) meaning he drew in/kept his head close (a gesture of embarrassment or restraint). و یک پوشه چاک = 'and a cloak/pouch torn' (یک پوشه = a garment/pouch; چاک qualifies it as torn). بگرفت is simple past third person.
segments.s160-g78: بداد و نبود آگه از شرم و باک = past narration + existential negation. بداد here functions as a past tense verb 'he gave' (classical/dialectal past of دادن). و links the clauses. نبود is past negative/existential 'there was not' or 'was not aware'; آگه 'aware/knowledge' is its complement. از شرم و باک 'of shame and of fear' gives the content of what was absent. (Note: بداد is an archaic past form of دادن; نبود is past existence.)
segments.s160-g79: رخان سیاوش چو گل شد ز شرم = subject phrase + simile + cause. رخان is an archaic/poetic reference to the face/cheeks (here 'the cheeks/face of Siavash'); سیاوش is the possessor/name. چو گل شد is a simile 'became like a flower' (چو = like/as; شد = became). ز شرم 'because of/from shame' gives the cause. The clause describes a blush/softening of complexion from shame.
segments.s160-g80: بیاراست مژگان به خوناب گرم = verb + object + instrumental phrase. بیاراست is a past/simple perfective verb 'he adorned / he arranged' (آراستن) in older poetic form. مژگان 'the eyelashes' is the direct object. به is instrumental/locative 'with/with the help of', and خوناب گرم 'warm blood-water' is the instrument/content ('with warm blood'), an intense poetic image. Word order places the verb before object as is common in epic poetry.
segments.s160-g169: This is a nominative/nominal phrase addressing someone directly: تو (you) + بانوی شاهی (a royal lady). The coordinate و links a second epithet خورشید گاه ('sun of the time' / 'sun of the age'), where خورشید is a metaphorical noun and گاه ('time, age') follows it as a genitive/qualifier. Word order is typical Persian: address + titles separated by و.
segments.s160-g170: سزد is an impersonal present/fixed expression meaning 'it befits' or 'it is proper'. کز is the archaic contracted form of که از/که which introduces the content clause ('that/from which'). ناید here functions as an optative/negative subjunctive 'may not come' or 'should not come' (classical Persian), followed by the adverb بدینسان 'in such a way' and the noun گناه 'sin'. The whole clause says it is proper that such a sin should not proceed from you.
segments.s160-g171: وزان is a fused prepositional element 'and/from that' (here 'from that [place/seat]'). تخت is the noun 'throne' and برخاست is the past tense verb 'she rose/stood up'. با + خشم و جنگ links two nouns describing her manner ('with anger and with combat/rage'), where جنگ here denotes a violent, combative mood rather than a formal war.
segments.s160-g172: بدو is the clitic/prepositional phrase 'to him' (i.e., toward him). اندر here means 'upon/at' or 'into' (showing closeness), and آویخت is the past of آویختن used transitively/intransitively 'clung/fastened (to)'. سودابه is the subject/name; چنگ in poetic bodily context means 'claw/hand/fingernail' (i.e., she seized or scratched him). Word order places the prepositional 'to him' before the verb in classical style.
segments.s160-g173: بدو گفت is the periphrastic construction 'she said to him' (بدو = 'to him'; گفت here functions as the verb 'said'). من راز دل ('the secret of my heart') is a possessive/genitive phrase with monadic pronoun من placed before راز. پیش تو literally 'before you' means 'in your presence'—preposition/locative phrase follows the object in this poetic ordering.
segments.s160-g174: بگفتم is first‑person past 'I said / I had told' used here as reported speech continuation. نهان can be adjective or adverb 'secretly/hidden' modifying how she told it. از introduces the source/measure 'from / because of,' followed by بداندیش 'ill‑disposed / malicious (mind[s])' and the pronominal تو which here likely refers back to the addressee or to malicious thoughts connected to him. The clause explains that the telling was done secretly because of hostile minds.
segments.s160-g175: مرا (object 'me') is split in the list as م + را; grammatically this is the accusative object. خیره خواهی uses خیره (from خیره شدن/کردن) meaning 'to astonish, to dazzle, to make stupefied' and خواهی is the 2nd person singular present/future 'you will/would', so 'will you render me astonished'. که introduces the subordinate result/purpose clause 'that you will ...', followed by رسوا کنی 'you disgrace/expose (me)' where کنی is the 2nd person subjunctive/imperative form of کردن used after که to express the action.
segments.s160-g176: به پیش is the locative phrase 'before / in the presence of'. خردمند رعنا is a compound epithet: خردمند 'wise' followed by رعنا 'graceful/beautiful' (both describe the people or audience). کنی is the same 2nd person singular present/subjunctive of کردن 'to do/make' and completes the verb phrase 'you will do (this) before the wise and graceful.'
segments.s160-g65: This small noun phrase means “a certain girl not yet mature.” 'یکی' is an indefinite premodifier meaning “a certain/one,” followed by the noun 'دختری' (girl). The adjective 'نارسیده' follows the noun (postpositive), giving “not-yet-grown/immature.” The token 'بجای' here marks that she is to be placed “in place (of someone),” preparing the object role for the verb that follows.
segments.s160-g66: 'کنم' is the first-person singular present/subjunctive/form of 'کردن' used here as the main verb meaning “I will make/place.” What follows ('چون پرستار پیشت به پای') states the role she will have: 'چون' = “as,” 'پرستار' = “nurse/caretaker.” 'پیشت' (literally “before you/in your presence”) plus 'به پای' (“at the foot/of your feet”) form a prepositional phrase describing service — i.e., “to serve at your feet.”
segments.s160-g67: 'به سوگند' is a prepositional phrase meaning “by oath” (the 'به' here = “by”). 'پیمان کن' is the imperative: “make a pledge / take a covenant.” 'اکنون' = “now,” and 'یکی' here introduces a single injunction or item (“one [thing]”), i.e. the speaker is about to state one thing the addressee must do.
segments.s160-g68: 'ز' is the poetic form of 'از' meaning “from.” 'گفتار من' = “my speech / my words” with the possessive 'من.' 'سر مپیچ' is a negative imperative: 'مپیچ' = “do not turn/swerve” where 'سر' (literally “head”) is used idiomatically (“turn your head away,” i.e. change your mind). 'اندکی' (“a little”) limits the negation: “not even a little.”
segments.s160-g69: 'چو' introduces a temporal clause meaning “when.” 'بیرون شود' is the verbal phrase “goes out / passes away” (literally “becomes out”), and 'زین جهان' = “from this world” (an older contraction of 'از این جهان'); 'شهریار' is the subject, “the king/sovereign.” The verb before the subject is a common word order in Persian subordinate clauses.
segments.s160-g70: 'تو خواهی' is the second-person future/intentional: “you will wish/ask for.” The object phrase follows: 'بدن زو' = “the body from him” (classical contraction of 'از او'). 'مرا یادگار' = “(as) a memento for me,” where 'مرا' (here split in the tokenization as 'م' + 'را') is the first‑person object marked by 'را' and 'یادگار' is the noun “keepsake/memento.”
segments.s160-g71: 'نمانی' is the negative/optative form of 'ماندن' used as a prohibition/plea — “do not remain / do not be absent.” 'که' introduces the subordinate clause “that/when,” and 'آید' is the present/subjunctive “comes.” 'به من' = “to me,” and 'بر گزند' = “upon harm” (the preposition 'بر' + noun 'گزند' meaning ‘harm’), giving: “do not be absent so that harm comes upon me.”
segments.s160-g72: 'بداری' (from داشتن) here is a hortative/imperative sense “keep/hold (me).” The object 'مرا' is split in the tokenization ('م' + 'را'); 'را' is the definite object marker. 'همچو او ارجمند' is a comparative clause: 'همچو او' = “like him” and 'ارجمند' = “esteemed/precious,” so the speaker asks to be kept as honored/valued as that other person.
segments.s160-g177: The verb بزد (past 'he struck') precedes its object (دست) — a common poetic word order. جامه بدرید is a simple past clause 'tore the garment'; پاک here is adverbial 'completely/cleanly' modifying بدرید.
segments.s160-g178: به ناخن expresses instrumentality ('with his nails') using به; دو رخ را is the definite object (را marks 'both cheeks'). همی کرد is the past (poetic) periphrasis with همی as a verbal particle giving an ongoing/attentive sense and کرد 'made/did'; چاک is the result noun 'a gash/tear'.
segments.s160-g179: برآمد is the past tense 'arose/issued forth' and stands before the noun خروش ('a cry') in poetic inversion. از شبستان qualifies the source ('from the bedchamber'), and اوی is an archaic enclitic possession meaning 'his/her' attached to شبستان.
segments.s160-g180: فغانش is فغان ('lament/cry') with the enclitic -ش 'his/her' — 'his lament'. ز (poetic form of از) marks origin 'from the iwan'; برآمد again is 'rose/issued forth'; به کوی gives the direction/destination 'into the quarter/street'.
segments.s160-g181: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain' or 'one' emphasizing a single event; غلغل 'clamor/uproar' is the noun that follows. از باغ و ایوان gives the sources 'from the garden and the porch'; بخاست is the past 'arose' (poetic past form).
segments.s160-g182: که introduces a result/simile clause ('so that/as if'); گفتی here functions impersonally ('one would say' / 'it seemed to say') rather than literal 'you said'. شب is the subject of the reported clause; رستخیزست is a predicate-noun 'is resurrection/resurrection-like upheaval'; راست is an intensifier 'true/indeed.'
segments.s160-g183: به گوش سپهبد is a prepositional phrase ('to the ear of the spahbed') placed before the verb for emphasis; رسید (past) means 'reached' or 'arrived' and takes آگهی ('news/information') as the item that arrived.
segments.s160-g184: فرود آمد is a common compound meaning 'he descended/alighted' (فرود + آمد). از تخت شاهنشهی gives the source/place 'from the shah's (imperial) throne' — تخت 'throne' modified by شاهنشهی 'imperial'.
segments.s160-g41: ازین is the contracted/poetic combination از + این (= 'of/from these'); خوب رویان = 'the good/beautiful-faced ones' (an epithet); بچشم is the poetic spelling of به چشم ('with the eye of') where the prefixed ب- corresponds to classical به; خرد here is a noun meaning 'wisdom' used instrumentally ('with the eye of wisdom'). Word order is noun phrase (of-these + epithet) followed by the instrumental phrase.
segments.s160-g42: نگه کن is an imperative: نگه (gaze/watch) + کن (do) = 'look/watch!'; the following که introduces the subordinate clause ('that/so that' or here more loosely 'beware that…'); با تو = 'upon/for you'; اندر خورد is a poetic periphrasis where اندر = 'in/at' and خورد is used in the sense 'befall/happen' — together 'what may befall you'. Note the double که is stylistic/poetic rather than changing meaning.
segments.s160-g43: فرو ماند is a poetic compound: فرو (down/deeply) + ماند (remained) = 'was cast down/was deeply affected/was left (in that state)'; و links clauses; پاسخ نداد is simple past negative: 'gave no answer' (نداد = did not give).
segments.s160-g44: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'; آمدش is آمد + enclitic -ش 'to him/for him' = 'came to him'; بر دل‌پاک = 'upon (his) pure heart' (بر = on/upon); یاد = 'memory/remembrance' and functions as the noun that 'came' — literal order 'thus came-to-him upon pure-heart remembrance'.
segments.s160-g45: که introduces a content clause 'that…'; من is the explicit subject 'I'; بر دل‌پاک = 'for/on the pure-hearted one' (lit. 'upon the pure heart'); شیون کنم uses the present/subjunctive form کنم after شیون to express intention/optative 'that I should wail' or 'I will wail' — classical/poetic mood rather than simple indicative.
segments.s160-g46: به آید is an impersonal phrase 'it may come/it may happen' (آید = come/happen); که introduces the subordinate content; از دشمنان = 'from/of the enemies' (i.e. at the hands of the enemies); زن کنم here is read as 'be given as a زن (wife)' with کنم in the subjunctive/future sense 'I may be made/given' — the verb phrase expresses the speaker's fear that she might be handed over to the enemies. (Note: زن is ambiguous in Persian, but in this line it functions as 'woman/wife'.)
segments.s160-g47: شنیدستم is an archaising past form 'I heard / I was told' (compound/poetic); از introduces the source 'from'; نامور مهتران = 'renowned mahterān' — مهتران is an old word for elders/chiefs (archaic plural), so 'from renowned chiefs/elders.'
segments.s160-g48: همه = 'all/every'; داستانهای = 'the stories/tales of'; هاماوران here is a place-name/toponym (Hamâvarân) and the whole phrase means 'all the tales of Hamâvarân' (the ezāfe linking is implied in the compound).
segments.s160-g369: This is a noun phrase: 'همه' (quantifier) precedes the plural noun 'روزبانان' (heralds). 'درگاه شاه' is a genitive/locative phrase 'the court of the king' placed after the noun in a poetic, appositive order, so the whole phrase means 'all the heralds of the king's court.'
segments.s160-g370: 'بفرمود' is the honorific/polite past 3rd‑person verb meaning 'he commanded/said.' The particle 'تا' here introduces the result or subsequent action ('so that' / 'and then'), and 'برگرفتند' is past 3rd plural 'they took up' (here 'set out' or 'took the road'). 'راه' is the direct object of برگرفتند (the path/road).
segments.s160-g371: 'همه شهر و برزن' is a coordinated noun phrase ('the whole city and the square/village‑place') meaning 'everyone in town and the square.' The prepositional phrase 'به پای' ('to the foot / on foot / to the feet') combines with the verb 'آورند' (past 3rd plural 'they brought/came')—here describing that the populace came and gathered at (the court's) feet or came on foot.
segments.s160-g372: This clause has the object followed by a verb: 'زن بدکنش' (the woman described as 'bad‑doing' or ill‑behaved), with 'را' marking her as definite object. 'بجای آورند' is the verb complex 'they brought (her) to the place/forward' — 'بجای' here means 'into the place / forward / before (the court)'.
segments.s160-g373: 'به نزدیکی اندر' is a locative phrase meaning 'close by / nearby within' (poetic order: preposition + noun + 'اندر' = 'in/at'). 'نشان یافتند' is a compound verb: 'نشان' (a mark/spot) + 'یافتند' (past 3rd plural 'they found/observed'), so together 'they found (her) near the mark/spot.'
segments.s160-g374: 'جهان دیدگان' is a poetic collocation: 'جهان' (the world) modifying 'دیدگان' (the eyes/gazes) — i.e., the onlookers' eyes. 'نیز' is the adverb 'also/likewise,' and 'بشتافتند' is past 3rd plural 'they hastened/ran.' The clause means that the crowd/onlookers likewise hurried toward the place.
segments.s160-g375: 'کشیدند' is past 3rd plural of 'کشیدن' ('they pulled/dragged'). The noun phrase 'بدبخت زن را' is the object ('the wretched/unfortunate woman' with 'را' marking definiteness). 'ز راه' is a prepositional phrase 'from the road / off the path' (poetic 'ز' = 'از'). Together: 'they dragged the wretched woman off the road.'
segments.s160-g376: 'به خواری' is an adverbial phrase 'in humiliation / to disgrace.' 'ببردند' is past 3rd plural of 'بردن' ('they took/carried'), and 'نزدیک شاه' ('near the king') is the goal/location. Word order places the adverbial before the verb phrase: 'they (in humiliation) brought her near the king.'
segments.s160-g265: و دیگر بدانگه که در بند بود = 'And afterwards, at that time when he was in bonds.' و begins the clause as a simple conjunction. دیگر here has the sense of 'other/another' used idiomatically as 'afterward' (an adverbial timing), and بدانگه is a temporal adverb meaning 'at that moment/thereupon.' که introduces a relative/subordinate clause ('when/that'), در + بند is a locative phrase 'in bondage' (بند = fetter, bond), and بود is the past copula 'was.'
segments.s160-g266: بر او نه خویش و نه پیوند بود = 'for him there was neither kin nor tie.' بر او is a prepositional phrase 'upon/for him' (بر used with a dative/experiential sense), نه ... و نه is the correlative negation 'neither ... nor,' خویش is a contracted/poetic form meaning 'his own/kin' (خوی + possessive clitic -ش), پیوند means 'bond/tie' (here social/familial tie), and بود is the past copula 'was' completing the existential sense.
segments.s160-g267: پرستار سودابه بد روز و شب = 'Soudabeh's nurse (suffered) badly day and night.' The noun phrase is head + possessor (پرستار 'nurse' followed by the name سودابه to show whom she tended). بد here is an adverbial/qualifier 'badly/miserably,' and روز و شب is a common adverbial pair meaning 'day and night.'
segments.s160-g268: که پیچید ازان درد و نگشاد لب = 'for that pain twisted her and (so) she did not open her lips.' که here gives a causal/explanatory link ('for/because'). پیچید is a past intransitive verb 'twisted/turned' (poetic for 'writhed' or 'was convulsed by'), ازان = از + آن 'from that' ('because of that'), درد is 'pain,' and نگشاد is negated past form ن- + گشاد (گشودن 'to open') meaning 'did not open' (negation prefix ن- on the past stem), with لب 'lip' as the object/thing not opened (i.e., she did not speak).
segments.s160-g269: سه دیگر که یک دل پر از مهر داشت = 'Three others who had one heart full of love.' سه دیگر = 'three others' (دیگر = 'other'), که introduces the relative clause 'who,' یک دل = 'one heart' (an idiom for unity/one mind), پر ... از is the construction 'full of X' (پر = full, از = 'of'), مهر = 'love/affection,' and داشت is the past verb 'had.'
segments.s160-g270: ببایست زو هر بد اندر گذاشت = 'they had to (or were obliged to) put/take every evil from him into themselves.' ببایست is an archaic/poetic modal 'it was necessary / they had to.' زو is poetic contraction (ز + او) meaning 'from him' (ز = from; the clitic او/و = 'him'), هر بد = 'every evil/hardship,' اندر گذاشت literally 'placed inside' (اندر = in/within, گذاشت = past 'put/placed'). The clause expresses obligation to bear each hardship on his behalf.
segments.s160-g271: چهارم کزو کودکان داشت خرد = 'The fourth, from whom there were children small' (i.e., 'the fourth, who had little children'). چهارم is the ordinal 'the fourth.' کزو = از + او 'from whom' (introducing possession/source), کودکان داشت = 'had children,' and خرد 'small' is an adjective placed after the clause for poetic emphasis (word order is inverted for metre; خرد qualifies کودکان = 'little children').
segments.s160-g272: غم خرد را خوار نتوان شمرد = 'One could not reckon the small/child's grief as despicable.' غم خرد = 'the grief of the small/children' (خرد = 'small' modifying غم), را is the definite/direct-object/contrast marker, خوار here means 'despised/worthless' (predicate adjective), نتوان is the negative potential 'could not,' and شمرد is the infinitival/past stem 'to count/consider.' Together they form 'could not count as worthless/insignificant.'
segments.s160-g145: ز هر چیز چندان که اندازه نیست is a classical, somewhat elliptical clause meaning 'from each thing (only) as much as is not beyond measure.' ز + هر چیز marks source/domain ('from every thing'); چنان (چندان) + که introduces a comparative/extent clause ('to the extent that') and اندازه نیست uses اندازه (measure, worth) with نیست as a negation, yielding the sense 'more than the measure' or 'beyond what is proper.'
segments.s160-g146: This is a conditional: اگر introduces 'if'. بر is the preposition 'upon/on'. نهی here is an archaic/poetic form; in this context it functions as a verb meaning 'you put/place' (classical usage) rather than the listed senses 'prohibition' or 'negative imperative' (so the provided dictionary senses do not match exactly). پیل is the noun 'elephant' and باید is a modal 'must/ought/it would be necessary,' while دویست (not listed among the provided words) is the numeral 'two hundred.' The clause therefore says that if you place an elephant on it, the measure needed would be two hundred.
segments.s160-g147: This is a slightly inverted, classical future construction: داد خواهد همی دخترم = 'he will give my daughter.' داد (give/gave) here is the verb root, خواهد is the future/auxiliary 'will', and همی is an emphatic/poetic particle (here stressing the action). به تو marks the indirect object 'to you' (to/for you), and دخترم is 'my daughter.'
segments.s160-g148: نگه کن is an imperative: نگه (watch/guard) + کن (do!) = 'keep watch' or 'look after.' بروی is the subjunctive/imperative-like form of رفتن 'to go' meaning 'you go' or 'go (and)...', linking actions; و is 'and', سر is 'head', و is again 'and', and افسرم here is the possessive 'my afsar' meaning 'my helmet' (classical word for helmet). The segment orders imperative and verbs in the classical compressed way.
segments.s160-g149: بهانه چه داری تو = 'what excuse do you have?' بهانه is 'pretext/excuse', چه is the interrogative 'what', داری is the present tense 'you have', and تو is the emphatic pronoun 'you.' از مهر من adds 'from my affection' (از = from/of, مهر = love/affection, من = my). The word order is typical classical Persian: question word + verb + pronoun, then the complement introduced by از.
segments.s160-g150: بپیچی is the 2nd/3rd-person subjunctive/optative form of پیچیدن 'to turn' here meaning 'you turn away' or 'you would turn'; the prefix بـ- marks the verb form used after a preceding verb or particle. ز بالا is 'from above' (بالا can mean 'high, exalted, lofty' — here likely 'from my high station/status'), و از چهر من adds 'and from my face/appearance' (چهر = face/appearance). The clause lists two things the addressee is turning away from.
segments.s160-g151: که تا من ترا دیده‌ام برده‌ام is a causal/temporal clause: که introduces 'for/that', تا here funcionan as a temporal 'since/as long as', من 'I', ترا (ت + را) is the object 'you' (the enclitic ت is the 2nd-person suffix + را object marker), دیده‌ام 'I have seen', and برده‌ام is perfect 'I have been carried/taken' (from بردن) used idiomatically for 'I have been swept away' or 'I have been transported' by feeling. Word order is classical: temporal subclause then result.
segments.s160-g152: This is a triad of participial/adjectival states describing the speaker's condition: خروشان 'roaring/turbulent', جوشان 'boiling/seething', و آزرده‌ام 'and I am distressed' (آزرده‌ام is the perfect/adjectival form 'I am hurt/distressed'). The three words are linked by و to intensify emotional turmoil; the final -ام on آزرده marks the first-person.
segments.s160-g185: پراندیشه is an adjectival epithet used substantively here ('the pensive one'). از تخت زرین is a prepositional phrase ('from the golden throne') placed after the subject; برفت is the past tense, third-person singular of رفتن with a prefixed بر- often found in classical/poetic Persian, meaning 'went away/left'. The clause order is Subject (adjectival substantive) + prepositional source + verb.
segments.s160-g186: به سوی شبستان is a directional phrase ('toward the bedchamber') that modifies the following motion verb. خرامید is past (3rd sg.) of خرامیدن, a stative/iterative past meaning 'strode/pranced proudly'; it is the main verb governing the direction phrase. تفت is unattached here to the expected verbal meaning and does not match the provided lexical senses (see senses map).
segments.s160-g187: بیامد is a past-tense verb 'he came'. چو introduces a subordinate temporal clause here ('when'). سودابه را uses the definite/direct-object marker را attached to the proper name; دید is the transitive past tense 'saw' taking روی ('face') as its object that is then described by the next segment.
segments.s160-g188: خراشیده is an adjective 'scratched/wounded' modifying the previously mentioned روی. The conjunction و links this clause to a new nominal clause: کاخ پر گفت و گوی literally 'the palace full of گفت و گو (talk/gossip)'. پر functions as a predicative adjective 'full (of)', and گفت و گوی is a compound meaning 'conversation/talk' (here 'gossip').
segments.s160-g189: ز هر کس بپرسید is a past clause 'he asked every/each person', where ز (poetic variant of از) marks source/agent consulted and بپرسید is past tense 'asked'. و شد تنگ‌دل links a resulting state: شد is the copulative/causative 'became' and تنگ‌دل is a predicate adjective meaning 'narrow‑hearted' or 'dejected' depending on context.
segments.s160-g190: ندانست is past negative 'he did not know' (ن- prefix + دانست). The object of not knowing is کردار آن سنگ دل: کردار 'deed/action', with آن as the demonstrative determiner 'that' modifying the compound سنگ دل 'stone‑hearted/hard‑hearted (person)'; the noun phrase is the direct object of ندانست.
segments.s160-g191: خروشید is a past verb meaning 'made an outcry/roared/cried out' and here is predicated of سودابه, which appears immediately after as the subject. در پیش اوی is a locative/relational phrase 'in his presence' where اوی is an archaic/enclitic form of the third-person pronoun 'him/he'.
segments.s160-g192: همی is a classical Persian marker of continuous or iterative aspect ('was ...ing'). ریخت here is used in the sense of 'to pour' or 'to shed' (she 'poured' water), with آب as its object. The conjunction و links the second simultaneous action: همی کند موی, where کند is the verb (from کندن) 'she would pull/tear (hair)' and موی 'hair' is its object; the same subject (Sudabeh) performs both ongoing actions.
segments.s160-g241: ز بس رنج کشتنش نزدیک بود is a compact causal clause: ز + بس = “because of so much,” رنج = “suffering,” and کشتنش is the verb کشتن with the third-person object suffix -ش (literally “killing him” / “his being killed”). نزدیک بود is the copula (بود) with the adjective نزدیک ‘near’; poetically the clause means ‘his death/killing was near because of so much suffering.’
segments.s160-g242: جهان پیش من تنگ و تاریک بود is a simple predicate with predicative adjectives. پیش من = ‘before/in front of me’ (پیش as a local/prepositional word), تنگ و تاریک are coordinate adjectives, and بود is the past copula: ‘the world before me was narrow and dark.’
segments.s160-g243: چنین گفت با خویشتن شهریار is an inversion common in poetry: چنین = ‘thus,’ گفت is the past of گفتن ‘said’ (here used with the meaning ‘said’ though the provided senses list does not show that sense), با خویشتن = ‘to himself,’ and شهریار is the speaker ‘the king.’ The order places the adverb and reflexive phrase before the subject.
segments.s160-g244: که گفتار هر دو نیاید به کار introduces a subordinate clause: که = ‘that’ (introducing the content), گفتار = ‘speech/words,’ هر دو = ‘both’ (a fixed combination meaning ‘the two of them’), نیاید به کار = ‘does not come to use / is of no use’ (نیاید is an older/poetic present/subjunctive-negative form meaning ‘does not help’).
segments.s160-g245: برین کار بر نیست جای شتاب is poetically inverted relative to prose order. برین = בר + این (poetic ‘on/for this’) modifies کار ‘this affair’; جای شتاب = ‘place for haste’; نیست = ‘is not/there is no.’ A more neutral order: «برای این کار جای شتاب نیست.»
segments.s160-g246: که تنگی دل آرد خرد را به خواب gives the reason clause: که = ‘because,’ تنگی دل = ‘the constriction/oppression of the heart’ (تنگی here = emotional distress), آرد = ‘brings’ (archaic/present form of آوردن used in poetry), خرد را = ‘wisdom/mind’ marked by the object marker را, and به خواب = ‘into sleep’ (i.e. ‘puts wisdom to sleep’).
segments.s160-g247: نگه کرد باید بدین در نخست shows poetic inversion: نگه کردن = ‘to keep watch’; کرد here is the verb part of that compound, and باید = ‘must/ought to.’ بدین در = ‘at this gate’ (بدین = به + این in older/poetic spelling) and نخست = ‘first.’ In prose order: «باید نخست بدین در نگه کرد.»
segments.s160-g248: گواهی دهد دل چو گردد درست is a conditional/time clause: گواهی دهد = ‘let/generally [it] give testimony’ where دهد is a present/subjunctive-like form used for a general injunction, دل = ‘the heart,’ چو = ‘when,’ گردد = poetic form of گشت/شود ‘becomes,’ and درست = ‘right/sound.’ Together: ‘the heart should give its testimony when it becomes sound.’
segments.s160-g89: همان به که here is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'it is better that' or 'better to...'; که introduces the following clause. با او is the prepositional phrase 'with/to him' (با + pronoun او) and به آواز نرم is another prepositional/adverbial phrase 'in a soft/gentle voice' (به + noun + adjective). Word order: prepositions precede their objects as usual.
segments.s160-g90: سخن گویم places the object noun سخن ('speech, words') before the verb گویم; گویم is 1st person present/subjunctive form of گفتن used here after که to express the speaker's intended action ('that I speak' / 'I should speak'). دارمش is دارم + enclitic -ش ('I keep/have him/it'), where the pronoun is attached; چرب و گرم are two adjectives joined by و describing the manner ('flattering and warm').
segments.s160-g91: ازان پس is a temporal phrase 'after that / thereafter' (از + آن + پس). به سودابه marks the indirect object with the preposition به ('to Sudabeh'). گفت is the 3rd person past verb 'said' (simple past) following the subject سیاوش.
segments.s160-g92: که introduces an indirect question. اندر جهان خود = 'in the world itself / in your own world' (اندر = in, خود as an intensifier/possessive emphasis). تراکیست جفت is an archaic/fused word order for ترا کیست جفت — تَرا is the object 'for you', کیست (archaic 'who is') forms the verb, and جفت ('match, pair') is the predicate: 'who is a match for you?'.
segments.s160-g93: نمانی is the negative 2nd-person form meaning 'you will not remain / you will not stay'. مگر here means 'unless' / 'except' introducing the limiting condition. نیمه ماه را is the object phrase 'the half of the moon' (را marks definiteness/emphasis) used metaphorically ('as a half-moon').
segments.s160-g94: نشایی is an infinitive/subjunctive-like form meaning 'you sit / you appear' (here the exact lexical sense is archaic; marked as uncertain). به گیتی is locative 'in the world' (به + گیتی). به جز is the compound 'except', and شاه را ('the king' + object marker را) emphasizes that exception — 'except the king'. The clause order places the locative and the exception after the verb for poetic effect.
segments.s160-g95: کنون دخترت بس = 'now your daughter is enough' (کنون = now, دخترت = your daughter, بس = enough). که begins a subordinate clause 'that ...'. باشد is the present/subjunctive 3rd-person form 'may be / be', and مرا (here segmented as م + را) is the object 'to me / mine' with را marking it; together 'که باشد مرا' means 'that she be mine'.
segments.s160-g96: نشاید is an impersonal/personal negative meaning 'it is not fitting / should not be'. به جز او = 'except her' (به جز = except, او = she/her). که باشد مرا repeats the relative/subjunctive clause 'that she be mine' (باشد = subjunctive 'be', مرا = 'to me/mine'); the whole clause denies any right to anyone but her. The grammar uses نشاید + subordinate clause to express what ought not to be.
segments.s160-g305: Word order: location/description first then the copula in past. 'دو بچه' = 'two children' (subject). 'چنان' + 'چون' together give a demonstrative/qualifying sense 'thus/so' or 'such as', and 'بود' is the past copula 'were'. 'دیوزاد' is a compound adjective/noun meaning 'demon‑born' that modifies the children.
segments.s160-g306: 'چه گونه' is an interrogative phrase meaning 'what sort / in what manner'. 'بود' is again the past copula 'was/were'. 'بچه جادو نژاد' is a noun phrase: 'بچه' (child) modified by the compound 'جادو‑نژاد' = 'of magic lineage' or 'magic‑bred'. The segment asks about the character/lineage of that particular child.
segments.s160-g307: 'نهان کرد' is a compound verb: 'نهان' (secret/hidden) with 'کرد' (did/made) = 'hid/secreted'. 'زن را' is marked with the object/topic marker 'را' (here indicating the definite person involved). 'و' = 'and', 'او خود' is emphatic 'she herself', and 'بخفت' is an archaic past form meaning 'slept'. The clause therefore reports a past action of hiding followed by the woman herself going to sleep.
segments.s160-g308: 'فغانش' = 'her lament/cry' (فغان + possessive -ش). 'برآمد' = 'arose/issued forth'. 'ز' (poetic form of 'از') = 'from', and 'کاخ نهفت' = 'the hidden/secret palace' (کاخ modified by نهفت). The clause describes the lament that came from the concealed palace.
segments.s160-g309: Typical locative order: 'در ایوان' = 'in the iwan/porch'. 'پرستار' = 'attendant/nurse' (here plural/collective sense), 'چندانک' is an old/poetic quantifier meaning 'so many / several' and 'بود' is the past copula 'there was/were'. The whole clause states that there were many attendants present in the iwan.
segments.s160-g310: 'به نزدیک' = 'towards/close to' (motion toward someone). 'سودابه' is the proper name. 'رفتند' is past 3rd‑person plural 'they went', and 'زود' here modifies the verb as 'quickly / soon'. Word order places the directional phrase before the verb and the adverb after it.
segments.s160-g311: 'یکی' = 'a certain one / someone'. Noun phrase 'طشت زرین' = 'a golden basin' (طشت = basin/tub, زرین = golden). 'بیارید' here is an archaic or poetic past/performative form meaning 'brought' (imperative forms exist but context shows narrative past: 'someone brought'). 'پیش' = 'before / in the presence of / forward' indicating the basin was brought forward/in front.
segments.s160-g312: 'بگفت' = 'he said / spoke'. 'آن سخن' = 'that speech / those words'. 'با' here functions as 'to / with' in the sense 'said to'. 'پرستار خویش' = 'his own attendant/nurse' formed from 'پرستار' (attendant) + 'خوی' (root for 'own/kin') + possessive suffix 'ش' = 'his'. The clause says he addressed those words to his attendant.
segments.s160-g105: مرا آفریننده از فر خویش = 'مرا' (me) is fronted for emphasis and followed by the agent noun آفریننده (the Creator). از فر خویش is a prepositional phrase 'out of his own فر' (فر = divine/royal radiance), where خویش+ش marks possession 'his own.' The whole segment works as the subject-plus-phrase that will be completed by the verb in the next segment.
segments.s160-g106: چنان آفرید‌ای نگارین ز پیش = چنان 'so/thus' modifies the verb آفرید (past tense 'created/fashioned'). نگارین ('beautiful/painted') is a predicative adjective describing the result. ز پیش ('from before/in former times') is an adverbial phrase giving the sense 'as in earlier days' — the verb phrase therefore states that he made her so beautiful, as in the past.
segments.s160-g107: تو این راز مگشای و با کس مگوی = Two negative imperatives. مگشای is the negative imperative ('do not open/disclose') of گشودن used metaphorically for revealing a secret; مگوی is the negative imperative ('do not tell') of گفتن. The object این راز ('this secret') appears before the verb for emphasis; با کس 'with anyone' completes the prohibition.
segments.s160-g108: مرا جز نهفتن همان نیست روی = This is a somewhat idiomatic older construction: مرا (= 'for me' / 'as for me') marks the experiencer; جز نهفتن همان 'except hiding itself' is the subject phrase ('nothing except hiding'); نیست negates ('there is not/there is no other'). روی here (literally 'face') functions idiomatically to indicate a condition/option — overall: 'for me there is no option except concealment.'
segments.s160-g109: سر بانوانی و هم مهتری = سر ('head/chief') followed by بانوانی ('of the ladies/women') gives the speaker's title/status. و هم مهتری adds 'and also (their) chief/preeminence' — an appositive phrase reinforcing the claim to rank. The predicate 'I am' is omitted here and understood from context.
segments.s160-g110: من ایدون گمانم که تو مادری = من 'I' begins the clause; ایدون is an archaic/poetic word meaning roughly 'at this sight/so appearing' or 'upon this' (literally 'coming/appearing'), functioning adverbially to set the occasion for گمانم ('my conjecture/I suppose'). که introduces the content clause 'that you are a mother' (تو مادری), where مادری is predicative 'as a mother.'
segments.s160-g111: بگفت این و غمگین برون شد به در = بگفت ('she said') takes the object این ('this') immediately after it in an older word order. و غمگین ('and sorrowful') is a parenthetical/adverbial adjective describing her state; برون شد به در is the verbal phrase 'went out to the door' where شد ('became') combines with برون to form the intransitive 'went out.'
segments.s160-g112: ز گفتار او بود آسیمه سر = ز گفتار او ('from/because of her speech') gives the cause; بود ('was') is the past copula and آسیمه سر is a fixed descriptive phrase meaning 'distraught' or 'in headlong disorder' (آسیمه + سر forming a compound adjective). The clause states the effect of her words: she was distraught.
segments.s160-g193: چنین گفت is a formulaic opening 'thus he said'. کامد is an archaic past/present-form verb 'came/approached' placed before its subject (سیاوش) — classical Persian often places verb before subject. به تخت is a prepositional phrase 'to/onto the throne' (به + noun).
segments.s160-g194: برآراست is a classical perfect/preterite verb 'arranged/adorned' taking the direct object that follows (چنگ). و simply links the two verb phrases. برآویخت is a classical verb 'rose up / lifted / struck up' and سخت is an adverbial modifier 'firmly/forcefully' modifying that verb; the clause describes his action on the harp.
segments.s160-g195: که here introduces reported speech ('that...'). جز تو 'except you' is the exception phrase. نخواهم is the negative present/future of 'خواستن' ('I will not want/I do not wish'); کسی را uses کسی as the (indefinite) object with the object marker را. ز بن is a poetic locution 'from the root/from the foundation' (i.e. from the beginning/deeply).
segments.s160-g196: جز اینت 'except this' (اینت is an archaic/dialectal form 'this'). همی راند is a habitual/present action 'ever drives/forces' (همی marks continuity). باید سخن ('it is necessary [that] speech') expresses a modal necessity tied to speaking; together the clause means 'only this continually compels me to speak.' Note the inverted classical word order where the modal/nominal element (سخن) follows باید.
segments.s160-g197: که introduces explanation ('for/that'). از تست is a compact/archaic expression linking the cause to 'you' (تست is an older/clipped form related to تو + است/توِت); جان و دلم 'my soul and my heart' — note the possessive suffix -م on دل. پر ز مهر is the adjectival phrase 'full of love' (پر + ز + noun).
segments.s160-g198: چه is an exclamatory/interrogative 'what/how' introducing a rhetorical question. پرهیزی از من means 'a refraining/abstinence from me' (پرهیزی = avoidance). تو‑ای خوب‌چهر is a vocative address 'you, O fair‑faced one' (تو‌ای = تو + ای, used to call someone).
segments.s160-g199: بینداخت is a past verb 'threw/cast off' (here 'he threw off'). افسر is the object 'helmet'. ز marks the source 'from', and مشکین سرم is a noun phrase 'my dark (or musk‑scented) head' with the possessive suffix -م on سر; classical Persian places the adjective مشکین before the noun.
segments.s160-g200: چنین 'thus/so' links the result. چاک شد is a passive/perfect construction 'was torn' (چاک used with شد). جامه 'garment/robe' is the subject of the passive. اندر برم literally 'upon me' (اندر = in/on, برم = بر + ـم 'on me'), so the line means 'my robe was torn upon me.'
segments.s160-g353: نه ... پیداست is a negative predicate: نه (not) negates پیداست (is apparent). رازش (راز + -ش “its secret”) is the thing being predicated and follows the predicate in this poetic word order. درین آسمان is در + این (here written as درین) + آسمان = “in this sky/heaven.”
segments.s160-g354: نه introduces the second half of the correlative negation (“nor”). اندر زمین = “in/on the earth.” این شگفتی is the noun phrase “this wonder/marvel.” بدان is the older verbal form built from the prefix ب- + دان (to know) and here functions roughly as “is known” (impersonal/passive sense): “nor is this marvel known on earth.”
segments.s160-g355: نشان is the head noun “sign/mark.” The following adjectives/attributive words — بداندیش (ill‑minded, malicious) and ناپاک (impure/immoral) — modify a following زن (woman); the ezāfe linking is omitted in poetry, so this reads as “the sign of the malicious, impure woman.”
segments.s160-g356: بگفتند is past plural “they said” (or “it was said”). با شاه در انجمن is a prepositional phrase indicating the setting/presence: با شاه = “with/in the presence of the king” and در انجمن = “in the assembly/council.” Together: “they told the king in the assembly.”
segments.s160-g357: نهان داشت = “kept (it) hidden” (داشت + adjective/participle نهان yields an ongoing/past sense “kept hidden”). کاووس is the agent (Kavus). و connects the clauses. باکس نگفت is archaic/poetic here: نگفت is the negative past “did not say/speak”; باکس appears adverbial/qualifying (related to fear/hesitation in context) — its exact sense is obscure in the lemma list, so it functions here as an adverbial notion of fear/reluctance, producing “and (out of) fear he did not speak.”
segments.s160-g358: همی دارد/داشت is the older progressive/continuous auxiliary (همی = still/continually + داشت = was/kept), so همی داشت پوشیده = “kept (it) covered/hidden.” اندر نهفت uses اندر = “in/within” and نهفت (hidden/concealed) to stress that it remained tucked away; the sequence repeats the idea of concealment for emphasis.
segments.s160-g359: برین کار is بر + این + کار = “upon this affair/over this matter.” بگذشت is past “passed” (time passed). یک هفته نیز = “one week also / a week moreover” — the phrase states that a week elapsed concerning that business.
segments.s160-g360: ز جادو = “from/by magic” (ز is the poetic form of از). جهان را uses the object/ topical marker را with جهان (“the world”); برآمد is the past verb “arose/appeared.” قفیز is an obscure/rare noun (not further glossed here) and is the thing that “appeared” — so: “by magic a qafiz came into/appeared in the world.”
segments.s160-g201: پراندیشه شد is a predicative phrase: پراندیشه (pensive, ‘full of thought’) functions as the predicate adjective and شد is the past-tense auxiliary ‘became’. زان is the classical prepositional/relative particle 'from that' giving the cause: زان سخن شهریار = 'from that speech of the king' (سخن governs شهریار in genitive relation).
segments.s160-g202: سخن کرد is the light verb construction 'to speak/say' (سخن + کرد = 'spoke/said'). هرگونه را places 'any manner/kind' as a definite/accusative element (را after هرگونه), and خواستار is an adjectival/participial element 'desired' modifying/limiting that manner: literally 'he spoke the kind that he desired' → 'he spoke however he wished.'
segments.s160-g203: به دل گفت is an idiom meaning 'said to his heart' = 'he said to himself / thought inwardly' (به + دل as direction, گفت = 'said'). ار introduces a conditional 'if'; این راست 'this [be] true' (این modifying راست). گوید is the classical present/subjunctive form 'would say / is said' used here in the conditional clause, and همی is a classical particle adding habitual/emphatic aspect ('indeed/still' or 'would').
segments.s160-g204: وزین‌گونه is an adverbial phrase 'in this manner / so stately/so' introducing the manner; زشتی is the abstract noun 'disgrace / ugliness / moral vileness' as the thing not to be sought; نجوید is the present negative/optative form of 'to seek' (here 'would not seek'); همی again adds an archaic emphatic/habitual nuance. Word order places manner first, then the object, then the verb.
segments.s160-g205: سیاووش را سر is the direct object (را marks the definite/definite object: 'Siyavash's head'). بباید is the modal auxiliary 'must / it is necessary', followed by برید (the verb stem) to indicate the required action: 'Siyavash's head must be cut.' The construction expresses necessity (bebāyad + verb).
