segments.s160-g391: بگفتند باشاه is a reporting clause: بگفتند (past 'they said' / 'they told') takes باشاه ('to/with the king') as the indirect object. کاین زن چه گفت is an embedded clause introduced by کاین ('that/this'), functioning as the content of what was said: چه گفت = 'what (she) said.' Note that the subject of گفت is omitted and understood from context.
segments.s160-g392: جهان‌آفرین داند اندر نهفت is an independent comment: جهان‌آفرین ('the world‑creator' = God) is the subject, داند is the present tense 'knows,' and اندر نهفت ('in/within the hidden') qualifies what is known — effectively 'knows what is concealed.' The compact word order (verb after subject, adverbial phrase following) is typical of poetic diction.
segments.s160-g441: مگر introduces an exception/conditional here ('unless'); کاتش is a poetic/compound noun (کات + -ش) functioning as the agent/subject meaning a sharp/active seeker or discovery; تیز is an adjective modifying کاتش ('keen/sharp'); پیدا کند is a present/subjunctive-style verbal phrase 'finds/discovers' (کند is the third-person present/subjunctive/indicative auxiliary). Word order is poetic: conditional particle + agent phrase + verb.
segments.s160-g442: گنه کرده را is the definite object: گنه کرده = 'one who has committed a sin' (past participle + noun) and را marks it as the direct object; زود is an adverb 'quickly/soon' modifying the following verbal phrase; رسوا کند is the verb phrase 'makes (him) notorious/exposes (him)' with کند again the present/subjunctive/indicative auxiliary. Together this gives '(it) quickly exposes the sinner.'
segments.s160-g443: چنین پاسخ آورد is a fixed formula: چنین ('thus/so') + پاسخ ('answer/reply') + آورد ('brought/uttered/said'); سودابه is the speaker's name; پیش here means 'in the presence of' or 'before (him/the assembly)'. Word order places the manner (چنین) and the noun (پاسخ) before the verb آورد, then the speaker and the locus (پیش).
segments.s160-g444: که introduces reported speech ('that'); من is the first‑person subject 'I'; راست is an adverb/adjective here meaning 'true/truthfully'; گویم is the first‑person present/subjunctive of 'to say' ('I say/I speak') used to report her declaration; به گفتار خویش is a prepositional phrase 'in/with my own words' (به + گفتار), where خویش is the possessive 'one's own' formed from خوی + ş. The whole clause is Sūdābeh's assertion that she speaks truly in her own account.
segments.s160-g445: فگنده دو کودک نمودم بشاه is a compact, somewhat archaic poetic clause: فگنده is a verbal/adjectival form used here as 'having placed/presented' (poetic perfective); دو کودک 'two children/infants' is the direct object; نمودم is the first‑person past 'I showed/presented'; بشاه = 'to the king' (ب + شاه) meaning 'before the king' or 'to the shah'. Word order places the object before نمودم as is common in older Persian.
segments.s160-g446: ازین بیشتر is an adverbial phrase 'from this onward / any more than this' (از + این); کس is 'anyone/no one' depending on context (here negative scope follows); نبیند is third‑person present/subjunctive/optative of 'to see' used like 'may not see' or 'shall not behold'; گناه 'sin' is the object. The clause expresses a wish/statement that no one will henceforth witness further sin.
segments.s160-g447: سیاووش را marks Siyâvash as the patient/topic with را; کرد باید درست is a slightly inverted older order equivalent to باید درست کرد/باید درست شود — 'must be made right' or 'must be set right.' کرد is the verbal root 'to make/do,' باید is the necessity modal 'must/ought,' and درست is an adjective/adverb 'right/proper' describing the needed action. The clause expresses an obligation regarding Siyâvash's proper treatment.
segments.s160-g448: که introduces the explanatory clause 'because/that'; این 'this' refers to the person just mentioned (the guilty one); بد is 'bad/evil' describing the deed; بکرد is the simple past 'did' (he/she did); و is the coordinating 'and'; تباهی 'destruction/ruin' is the object; بجست is past of جستن here 'sought' (i.e., 'sought destruction' or 'brought about ruin'). The clause explains the reason: this one did evil and sought ruin.
segments.s160-g209: کسی را (kasi ra) is an indefinite noun marked by را (the object/definiteness marker). که introduces a relative clause 'who/whom'. اندر شبستان is a prepositional phrase 'in the inner chamber'. بدند is an archaic third-person plural past form (here linking the relative clause to what follows) and functions as the main verb of the clause.
segments.s160-g210: هشیوار و مهترپرستان is a coordinated nominal predicate ('alert/prudent and elder‑worshippers/flatterers'); the conjunction و links the two predicate nouns. بدند is the same past plural verb as above ('they were' / 'they judged (him) to be') and attaches this predicate back to the person indicated by کسی را.
segments.s160-g211: گسی is an indefinite pronoun 'someone/a certain one' and کرد is the past of کردن 'did/went'. و بر گاه is a temporal phrase 'and then/at that moment' (بر گاه = 'on the occasion'). تنها بماند is a past-form of ماندن 'remain/be left' with تنها 'alone' — together 'was left alone' or 'remained alone'.
segments.s160-g212: سیاووش و سودابه را is the coordinated direct object (names marked by را). پیش is an adverb/preposition 'before/in the presence of', and خواند is the past of خواندن here meaning 'he called/summoned'. Word order places the object before the verb, a common pattern in Classical Persian.
segments.s160-g213: به هوش و خرد is an adverbial phrase 'with awareness and wisdom' (به + nouns used adverbially). با سیاووش گفت places با + name after these adverbials and then گفت 'said/spoke' as the main verb; the speaker (subject) is omitted but implied.
segments.s160-g214: که introduces reported speech 'that...'. این راز 'this secret' is the topic; بر من نشاید is an idiom meaning 'it is not fitting for me' (نشاید = 'it does not become / is not proper'). نهفت here is the verbal/adjectival root meaning 'to hide/conceal' (i.e., 'it is not fitting that I hide this secret').
segments.s160-g215: نکردی تو این بد is a negative past addressed to 'you' (تو): 'you did not do this bad/evil (thing)'. که من کرده‌ام is a relative clause 'which I have done' (من = 'I', کرده‌ام = 'I have done'), so the whole clause says 'you did not do this evil that I have done.'
segments.s160-g216: ز introduces the cause/agent 'from/because of'; گفتار بیهوده is a noun phrase 'idle/useless speech' (بیهوده here = 'worthless/pointless'), and آزرده‌ام is the stative verb 'I am distressed/harried' (compound of آزرده + ام). The whole means 'I am pained/afflicted by idle talk.'
segments.s160-g225: بگفتم opens reported speech ('I said') and introduces the clause that follows. همه هرچ together function as 'all that / whatever' (همه intensifying هرچ), and شاه جهان is a nominative title 'the king of the world' (classical poetic order without an explicit ezāfe).
segments.s160-g226: بدو داد is a relative/attributive idea 'that the king gave to him' (بدو = 'to him'); خواست is the main past-tense verb 'he wanted/desired' placed toward the end as in classical Persian; آشکار و نهان are paired adverbial/qualifying words 'openly and secretly' describing the manner of wanting.
segments.s160-g227: Repeated ز is the poetic preposition 'from/with' (here functioning as 'with' or 'adorned with') introducing a list: ز فرزند 'with offspring', ز تاج 'with the crown'. وز here is a fusion of و + ز (= 'and with') used in poetry; خواسته is a noun 'wish/desire' (i.e. 'with desires').
segments.s160-g228: ز دینار وز گنج lists further adornments: ز دینار 'with dinars', وز گنج 'and with treasure' (وز again = و + ز). آراسته is a past-participial/adjectival predicate 'adorned' applied to the (omitted) subject; in poetry the participle comes after its complements.
segments.s160-g229: بگفتم که introduces a subordinate clause ('I said that ...'). چندین = 'several/many'. برین is the superlative/quality 'the best/the finest' (morphologically بر + -ین in the manuscript segmentation). بر نهم is the verb phrase 'I would place / I will set upon' — نهم is first-person present/subjunctive/intentional ('I put/I should put'), with the object placed before the verb as is common in poetic inversion.
segments.s160-g230: همه نیکویها = 'all good/excellent things' (نیکویها = plural 'the good ones'); به دختر = dative 'to/for the daughter/maiden'; (implicit) دهم is the first-person verb 'I give' placed at the end of the clause in the original line — the clause expresses the speaker's intention to bestow everything on the girl.
segments.s160-g231: مرا گفت = 'he said to me' (object before verb — an older/poetic order). با خواسته کار نیست literally 'with a request/desire there is no business/use' → idiomatically 'asking/pleading is of no use.' کار نیست = 'it is of no use / there is no matter', with نیست negating existence/effect.
segments.s160-g232: به دختر مرا = 'to/for my daughter' (دختر followed by the possessive/object marker represented here in the manuscript splitting as مرا); راه دیدار = 'the way/path of meeting' (a compound meaning 'a way to see/meet'); نیست negates existence: 'there is no way to see (my) daughter.' This is a negative-existential clause (راه ... نیست).
segments.s160-g449: Word order is inverted for emphasis: the preposition به + indirect object (به پور جوان) marks the addressee 'to the young son'; گفت is the simple past 'said' (3rd sing.); شاه زمین is the subject phrase 'the king of the land' (شاه modified by زمین). Together they mean 'the king of the land said to the young son.'
segments.s160-g450: که here introduces the content of the king's speech (a reported question). رایت is the noun 'standard/banner' (the thing being looked at). چه is the interrogative 'what'. بیند is the classical/persian present-subjunctive/indirect form of 'to see' used in reported questions after گفت. کنون = 'now'; اندرین = 'among/in these' (i.e. among these standards). Literally: 'that what the standard(s) sees now among these' → 'what do you see now among these standards?'.
segments.s160-g451: سیاوش is the speaker's name. چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' qualifying how he spoke. گفت is the past-tense 'said'. کای is a vocative particle 'O' addressing someone; شهریار is the vocative 'king/lord'. The sequence is a standard formula introducing direct speech: 'Siavash thus said, "O king..."'.
segments.s160-g452: که introduces the content of what was said ('that/because'). دوزخ = 'hell' (subject). م and را represent the pronoun مرا split across the list; grammatically مرا = 'to me' (dative/accusative). زین سخن = 'from this speech' (zīn = 'this' + سخن 'speech'), giving the cause. گشت is the past 'became/was turned'; خوار is a predicate adjective 'despised/mean/contemptible'. Together: 'that hell, because of this speech, became contemptible to me' (i.e. 'this speech made hell seem contemptible to me').
segments.s160-g453: اگر is the conditional 'if'. کوه آتش = 'mountain of fire' (noun phrase). بود is the past/subjunctive copula 'were/was' (here a counterfactual 'were'). بسپرم is 1st-person singular subjunctive/conditional form of سپردن (here used idiomatically: 'I would set/venture/commit (myself)' — i.e. 'I would face it'). The clause expresses a hypothetical willingness ('If the mountain were fire, I would ...').
segments.s160-g454: ازین is 'from this' (از + این) indicating the place/thing in question; تنگ = 'narrow' or 'narrow pass' (here a noun: narrow place). خوارست is a fused predicate: خوار + -ست (archaic 'is low/despicable') meaning 'is humiliating/despicable'. اگر بگذرم = 'if I pass' where بگذرم is the present-subjunctive/conditional of گذشتن 'to pass' (1st sing.). The sense: 'but to pass out of this narrow place would be humiliating.'
segments.s160-g455: پراندیشه is an adjectival compound 'full of thought / pensive'. شد is the past 'became'. جان کاووس = 'the life/soul of Kavus' (جان as the possessed noun, کاووس the possessor). کی here functions as 'when' (introducing the question/point of worry). The sequence means 'Kavus's soul became thoughtful/pensive, (asking) when...'.
segments.s160-g456: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of/about' indicating cause or topic. فرزند = 'the child/son' (here the relevant son). و = 'and'. سودابه is the female name Sudabeh. نیک‌پی is an epithet 'well-born / of noble descent' qualifying سودابه. Together: 'about the son and Sudabeh, the well‑born.'
segments.s160-g377: به خوبی is an adverbial phrase ('with kindness' / 'kindly') placed before the verb. بپرسید is Ferdowsi's past-third-person form 'he asked' (classical poetic use of the b‑prefix on past verbs). و connects the next clause; کردش is کرد + enclitic object ش ('he made/gave to her'), so کردش امید = 'and he gave/made for her (a) hope.'
segments.s160-g378: بسی modifies روز and here means 'many/much'; روز را داد is a transitive clause 'he gave many days' (روز را — definite object marker را highlights 'days' — داد = 'he gave'). نیزش is نیز + ش ('also' + her) so the phrase means 'and also gave her نوید'; نوید is the direct object meaning 'promise/omen' or 'good news' in this context.
segments.s160-g379: وزان پس is a temporal connective ('and from that / thereafter'); پس reinforces the sequence ('then'). The series به خواری و زخم و به بند uses به before nouns to mark state/condition ('in humiliation, and wounded, and in chains'), with و linking the nouns.
segments.s160-g380: به پردخت is a classical/past verbal phrase (پردخت here functioning like پرداخت/‘dealt with’ or 'handed over') introduced by به for direction ('they handed over to/led to'). از او functions as the indirect object phrase referring to the recipient ('to him' — i.e. the mentioned person), and شهریار بلند is the noun phrase apposition 'the lofty/high king' that receives the action. Overall: 'they delivered her to the lofty king.'
segments.s160-g381: نبد is the classical negative-past copula 'there was not / was not'. هیچ modifies خستو ('no one/none weary' — here خستو functions as a noun 'the weary one'). ب + دان combine as بدان ('in/at that'), so بدان داستان = 'in that tale/story'. The clause reads 'there was no one weary in that story.'
segments.s160-g382: Again نبد negates the following noun phrase: شاه پرمایه همداستان is a noun phrase where پرمایه qualifies شاه ('a king of substance/wealth'), and همداستان is a predicate/adjectival noun meaning 'an accomplice / like‑minded (one)'. The whole means 'there was no wealthy/substantial king who was an accomplice.'
segments.s160-g383: بفرمود is the honorific past 'he commanded/ordered'. کز is a subordinating particle ('that / from which' functioning here like 'that/from before'), پیش means 'before / in the presence (of him)', بیرون is 'outside/away', and برند (برد with a plural ending) is a third‑person plural form 'they take/carry away' used in the subordinate clause after the command: 'that they be taken out from before him.'
segments.s160-g384: بسی here means 'many/much' and modifies the infinitival/verblike phrases. چاره جویند is 'they seek a remedy' (جویند = they seek/search) — after an order this reads like an instruction 'let many seek means/remedies' — و links the next verb; افسون برند uses افسون 'charm/enchantment' as object and برند 'they bring/carry' so 'and let them bring enchantments.' The verbs are third plural, used as reported/indirect commands or instructions.
segments.s160-g481: گذر بود is a past nominal clause: گذر (passage) + بود (was). چنان که is a degree/result connector meaning “so … that”; گویی here is a parenthetical simile meaning “as if / it seemed” (a poetic use) introducing the following image; سوار is the logical subject ‘a rider’ placed after the simile for emphasis.
segments.s160-g482: میانه is an adverbial/locative meaning ‘into the middle’ or ‘to the midst’; برفتی is an archaic/epic past form meaning ‘rode/went’ (third‑person past) and carries the action; به introduces the locative ‘into/on/onto’ and تنگی چار is the place phrase ‘the narrowness (of the four)’ — overall ‘rode into the middle of the narrow place of the four’.
segments.s160-g483: بدانگاه is a temporal adverb ‘at that moment/then’. سوگند پرمایه is a noun phrase where پرمایه ('weighty, substantial') modifies سوگند ('oath'); شاه follows as the possessor (classical Persian genitive order: noun + possessor).
segments.s160-g484: This is a balanced declarative: چنین بود آیین = ‘the custom was thus’ (چنین + copula بود inverted for emphasis); و این بود راه = ‘and this was the way’ (این pointing to the foregoing). بود is the past copula linking subject and predicate.
segments.s160-g485: وزان پس is a classical connective meaning ‘and after that / from then on’; به موبد is the indirect object ‘to the mobed’; بفرمود is an honorific past verb meaning ‘he commanded/ordered/said’ and شاه (the subject) appears at the end in inverted epic order.
segments.s160-g486: که introduces the subordinate (content) clause introduced by the verb of command. بر چوب is a locative phrase ‘upon/on the wood’; ریزند here is a present/subjunctive-like form ‘(they) pour / should pour’ used after an order; نفط سیاه is the object ‘black oil’ (lamp oil).
segments.s160-g487: بیامد is a past tense verb ‘came’; دو صد مرد is the numeral phrase ‘two hundred men’ (number + noun); آتش‌فروز is a postpositive descriptive/epithet ‘fire‑lighter / incendiary’ describing those men. Word order here (verb then noun phrase) is common in epic narration.
segments.s160-g488: دمیدند is a past verb ‘they blew’ (likely horns or bellows). گفتی in this line functions idiomatically like a simile-maker ‘as if / it seemed’ (an archaic/poetic usage), introducing the comparison that follows. شب آمد به‌روز literally ‘night came to day’ = ‘night turned into day’ (به‌روز = into day/like day).
segments.s160-g433: ز هر در سخن sets up a locative/figurative phrase: ز + هر + در + سخن = 'from/in every door/topic of speech'. چُنان‌سازِ clause follows with چون ... گشت = 'when/as ... became'. بدین گونه is an adverbial phrase 'in this way'; گشت is the past-tense/stative verb 'became/turned'. Word order is typical older Persian: temporal clause introduced by چون before the verb گشت.
segments.s160-g434: This is an impersonal/modal clause: بر آتش (on/upon the fire) is the prepositional phrase, یکی را uses را as the definite/object marker marking 'someone' as the patient, and بباید + گذشت expresses necessity ('one must/ought to pass'). گذشت here functions as the verbal idea 'to pass' (infinitival/abstract verbal form). The order mirrors classical Persian: prepositional phrase, object, modal verb + main verb.
segments.s160-g435: چنین است is a copular/equative opening meaning 'thus is' or 'such is'. سوگند چرخ بلند is a genitive/possessive phrase 'the oath of the lofty sky/heavens' (چرخ بلند = high/heavenly), with modifier following the head noun in the classical word order.
segments.s160-g436: که introduces the subordinate clause explaining the oath. بر + [noun] + نیاید گزند is an impersonal construction meaning 'harm does not come upon/befall [the innocents]'; نیاید is a negative present/subjunctive-like form meaning 'shall not come' or 'does not befall'. بر here means 'upon', گزند = 'harm'.
segments.s160-g437: جهاندار is the subject 'the sovereign'; سودابه را is the direct object with را marking definiteness/patient. پیش خواند is a compound verbal expression 'called before / summoned (into his presence)' – خواند here means 'to call/summon' (archaic narrative past).
segments.s160-g438: همی is a narrative/discourse particle (here marking immediate action/continuation). با سیاوش بگفتن is an infinitival purpose phrase 'to speak with Siavash' (با = 'with', بگفتن = 'to speak/say'). نشاند is the past verb 'seated (her)' — archaic/perfective 'set down/caused to sit' taking the previously marked object.
segments.s160-g439: سرانجام گفت is 'finally said' (سرانجام = 'at last/finally'; گفت is the reported-speech verb 'said' in narrative). ایمن از هر دوان is a predicative phrase: ایمن از = 'safe from', هر دوان = 'every pursuit/assault/coming about' (دوان here = onrushs/pursuits).
segments.s160-g440: نگردد is the negative present/jussive form of گشت 'become' (here 'will not become / will not be made'), followed by مرا دل = 'my heart' where مرا is the older oblique/possessive form ('to/for me') and دل is 'heart'. روشن‌روان is a vocative epithet 'O bright‑souled one' addressing the addressee. The clause thus says the speaker's heart will not be secure from all pursuits, addressed to the bright‑souled person.
segments.s160-g393: به introduces the indirect object (‘to Sudabeh’). فرمود is the past tense verb ‘he commanded/said’ (3rd sing.). تا introduces the subordinate clause of purpose or commanded action (‘that she should …’). رفت is the past of رفتن ‘she went’. پیش here means ‘into the presence of / before’ (motion toward someone).
segments.s160-g394: ستاره + شمر here reads as a nominal phrase referring to the person before whom she goes (either an epithet + name or a name containing 'ستاره'). گفت is the past verbal idea ‘said’ (here used with گفتار خویش). گفتار خویش = ‘her/his speech’; خوی + ش are split orthographically (خویش = own), so خوی here combines with the enclitic ش to yield ‘own’.
segments.s160-g395: که introduces a reported/explanatory clause (‘that …’). این هر دو کودک = ‘these two children’ (هر دو = both). ز جادو = ‘by/from magic’; the sequence زنند here is an archaic/poetic cluster: زن (woman) + -ند forming the idea of a magic-woman or the action of enchantment — contextually this phrase means ‘produced by a sorceress’ or ‘the work of magic’.
segments.s160-g396: پدیدند is past plural ‘they appeared / were produced’. کز is a fused relative/ prepositional element meaning ‘from which / from’. پشت in poetic usage often indicates origin/lineage (‘the back/line’), here ‘of the stock of …’. اهریمنند is a predicate ‘they are of Ahriman / demonic’ (3rd pl. form).
segments.s160-g397: چنین = ‘thus/so’. پاسخ آورد is a common phrase ‘gave an answer / replied’; آورد is the past of آوردن and here functions as ‘uttered/related’ (reported speech). سودابه is the subject (proper name). باز = ‘again’ (here indicating repetition).
segments.s160-g398: که introduces the content of her reply (‘that …’). نزدیک ایشان = ‘near them / to those close to them’; جز اینست راز = ‘there is no secret except this’ (جز = except; اینست = ‘this is’; راز = secret). Together: she says the only secret pertaining to them is this.
segments.s160-g399: فزونستشان is a compound meaning ‘it grew/increased in them’ or ‘it made them more [so]’ (فزون = more; -شان = their/them). زین سخن = ‘from this speech’ (i.e. ‘because of these words’). در نهفت = ‘they hid it inwardly / became more secretive’ (در + نهفت — نهفت = hidden/concealed). Overall: the words made them hide the matter more.
segments.s160-g400: ز بهر سیاوش = ‘for the sake of Siyavash’ (ز/از = from/for; بهر = for; سیاوش = personal name). نیارند گفت is an archaic/elliptical phrasing meaning ‘they did not bring/say it’ or ‘they did not speak (of it)’; نیارند here is a negative/resulting verb-form and گفت completes the idea ‘say/speak’ (they refrained from saying it for Siyavash’s sake).
segments.s160-g497: هشیوار is an adjective/adverb meaning 'alert, awake' placed at the start to describe the subject's state. و is the simple coordinating conjunction 'and' linking this phrase to what follows. با + جامهای uses the preposition با 'with' plus جامهای (plural 'cups') to indicate accompaniment or what he holds; سپید postmodifies جامهای as an adjective 'white'. The whole segment is a compact descriptive clause ('alert and with white cups') that connects to the next segment as a continuing description of appearance and mood.
segments.s160-g498: لبی is a nominal form ('a lip' / 'his lips') used adverbially here as a short attributive image; پر ز is the idiom 'full of' (پر = 'full', ز = 'of/from' in poetic register). خنده is the noun 'laughter' or 'smile' filling the lip. دلی is a noun form meaning 'a heart' (here used as the subject of the adjective that follows), and پرامید is a compound adjective 'full of hope / hopeful'. Together the phrase strings two parallel images: 'a lip full of laughter, a heart full of hope.'
segments.s160-g499: یکی here = 'a certain / one' introducing a single figure. تازیی (Tazi) denotes a swift/fierce (Arab) horse or a spirited creature; it is adjectival/nominative. بر + نشسته uses بر 'on/upon' with نشسته 'seated' (past participle) to say the animal had reared up or taken a posture. سیاه postmodifies the noun as 'black.' The word order is compressed (determiner + noun + locative/participial phrase + adjective) as is typical in epic description.
segments.s160-g500: همی is an emphatic/iterative particle often used in epic Persian to mark action (here 'indeed/straightaway'); خاک نعلش = 'the dust of its horseshoe' (خاک 'dust', نعلش 'its horseshoe' with -ش = his/its). برآمد is a past tense verb 'arose/rose up'. به + ماه = 'toward/to the moon' (a poetic hyperbole). The clause describes the dust kicked up by the hoof rising high — a vivid image linked to the previous segment's action of the horse.
segments.s160-g501: پراگنده is an adjective/participle meaning 'scattered' or 'strewn.' کافور is the noun 'camphor' (a substance used in burial rites). بر خویشتن uses the preposition بر 'on/upon' with خویشتن 'himself' (reflexive/emphatic), so the phrase says 'camphor scattered upon himself.' This positions the detail as part of a funerary image tied to what follows.
segments.s160-g502: چنان چون is a paired expression ('such as / just as') used for emphasis: چنان 'so/such' + چون 'as/like'. بود is the past copula 'was.' رسم و ساز is a compound 'rite/custom (رسم) and arrangement/preparation (ساز)' — here referring to ritual practice — and کفن is 'shroud.' Grammatically this closes the image by saying the camphor-on-self matched the usual rite of a shroud.
segments.s160-g503: بدانگه 'thereupon/at that moment' begins a temporal clause. که introduces the subordinate clause 'when/that', شد is the past verb 'happened/became' used here as 'came into the state of being before', and پیش کاووس means 'in the presence of (before) Kay Kavus' (پیش = before/in the presence of + proper name). باز 'again' modifies the whole action ('once more / again'). Together the segment sets the time/context for the following actions.
segments.s160-g504: فرود آمد is a compound verb 'alighted / descended / dismounted' (فرود as noun/participle + آمد 'came' used as past). از باره literally 'from the burden/load' — here idiomatically 'from the saddle/load' (باره = load/burden) marking dismounting or leaving the conveyance. بردش is an older/simple past form of بردن 'to take/carry/lead' with enclitic object -ش 'him/it' (here 'led/took him'), and نماز is 'prayer' (in context the funeral prayer). The clause therefore describes dismounting and performing/taking the prayer for the person; word order is typical older Persian with verbs at the end.
segments.s160-g585: بفرمای is an honorific/imperative verb meaning a polite command or invitation ('do/please do'); it appears before the conjunction و which links to the speaker's personal statement. من دل نهادم برین is a past tense clause: من (I) + دل (heart) + نهادم (I placed/laid) + بر + این (here written enclitically as ین) meaning 'I set my heart upon this.' Note the enclitic ین attached to بر (برین = بر این), a common poetic orthography.
segments.s160-g586: نبود is the negative past copula meaning 'there was not'; the following nominal phrase آتش تیز ('a keen/sharp fire') is what does not exist. با او is a prepositional adjunct 'with him', and به کین 'for/with regard to vengeance' marks purpose/relation. Word order is typical poetic Persian: negative copula + noun phrase + adjuncts.
segments.s160-g587: سیاوش is the subject (proper name). سخن راست is the object phrase 'true speech' or 'truth'. گوید is the present/habitual verb 'he says/utters'; here it reports habitual or emphatic truth-telling, and همی is an aspectual particle signalling continuity or emphasis ('keeps / indeed'). Note the S–O–V order common in poetry: subject + object phrase + verb + aspect particle.
segments.s160-g588: دل شاه is a genitival phrase 'the king's heart' (noun + possessor). از غم is a prepositional phrase 'from grief', and بشوید is a present/optative form of 'شدن' (here 'become / be made'), meaning 'is washed away' or 'becomes rid of' in this context. همی again gives continuous or emphatic sense ('continually / will keep').
segments.s160-g589: همه جادوی زال is a noun phrase 'all (the) magic of Zal' with جادوی functioning as a genitive/attributive 'magic (of)'. کرد is the past tense verb 'did/made', and اندرین is an adverbial 'in this / in these (matters)' placed after the verb in poetic word order. The clause means 'Zal's magic did all this' or 'all this was the work of Zal's sorcery.'
segments.s160-g590: نخواهم is the negative present 'I do not want / I wish not'. که introduces a subordinate clause. داری دل ... is a collocation: داری (you have/harbor) + دل (a heart) + از من (toward me) + بکین (to take revenge / to be vengeful). In this construction دل داشتن + infinitive/verb expresses having the heart/intention to do something; so نخواهم که داری دل از من بکین = 'I do not want you to have a heart against me' → 'I do not wish you to bear a grudge or take vengeance on me.'
segments.s160-g591: بدو گفت is a light, idiomatic sequence meaning 'he said to him' (بدو = to him/unto him precedes گفت). نیرنگ داری هنوز is direct speech content: نیرنگ داری 'you have deceit/tricks' with داری as the present 'you possess/you practice', and هنوز 'still/yet' as an adverb modifying the continuing state.
segments.s160-g592: نگردد همی is a negative present/subjunctive construction meaning 'it does not turn / will not change' (نگردد = 'does not turn / will not become different') with همی giving continuous/emphatic sense. پشت شوخیت is a compact phrase: پشت (back) + شوخیت (your playfulness/impudence) — idiomatically referring to the stubborn habit or 'back' of your impudence — and کوز is a vocative or insult (an earthen jar/jug), used here as a form of address. The whole line is a taunt: 'your impudence will not change, o jar/Kōz.'
segments.s160-g489: نخستین دمیدن is a nominal phrase: the infinitive دمیدن ('to blow') is used as a noun meaning 'the first blow / first blast'. سیه شد is a two-word predicate: سیه 'black/dark' + شد (past of شدن) 'became' = 'became dark'. ز دود is an ablative/causal phrase 'from / because of smoke' modifying why it became dark. Word order is poetic (noun phrase then predicate then cause).
segments.s160-g490: زبانه برآمد is a simple past clause: زبانه 'a tongue (of flame)' as subject and برآمد 'arose/issued forth' as verb. پس از دود is a prepositional temporal phrase 'after/from the smoke' (پس = 'after' here, followed by the preposition از). زود is an adverb 'soon/quickly' modifying برآمد and placed after the temporal phrase for emphasis.
segments.s160-g491: زمین گشت روشنتر از آسمان is a comparative clause: زمین 'the earth' is subject, گشت (past of گشتن) means 'became', and روشنتر از آسمان is a comparative phrase 'brighter than the sky' (از introduces the standard of comparison).
segments.s160-g492: This is a nominal/elliptical clause that continues the scene: جهانی خروشان 'the whole/world in tumult' and آتش دمان 'fires at the skirts' are coordinated by و. There is no explicit verb here; Persian poetry often omits a repeated copula (it links back to گشت or simply describes the state). دمان (دامان) means 'skirts/edges' — 'fires along the skirts' of the world.
segments.s160-g493: سراسر همه is an intensifying adverbial phrase 'throughout / all across'; دشت 'the plains' is the affected place, and بریان شدند is a past passive/resultative 'were scorched / were roasted' (بریان = 'burnt, scorched' + شدند 'became/were'). The plural result aligns with the general quantity implied by سراسر همه.
segments.s160-g494: بران is an adverbial 'thereon / upon that / as a result'. چهر خندانش is a noun phrase 'their/his smiling faces' (خندان + possessive suffix -ش), and گریان شدند is a change-of-state past 'became weeping / were made to weep'. The line links the burned plains to the transformation of formerly smiling faces into sorrow.
segments.s160-g495: سیاوش بیامد is a simple past clause: the proper name Siyâvash + بیامد 'came'. به پیش پدر is a prepositional phrase 'to the presence of (his) father' — پیش here means 'in the presence of / before' and marks the addressee/location of the action.
segments.s160-g496: یکی خود زرین is an indefinite noun phrase: یکی 'a certain' + خود as emphatic/possessive ('his own' or 'himself') + زرین 'golden' (used substantively as 'a golden one'—implicitly a cap or ornament). نهاده به سر is a perfect participial construction 'having placed / placed on the head' (نهاده = 'placed', followed by the locative phrase به سر = 'on (his) head'), forming a circumstantial clause describing his appearance as he came.
segments.s160-g545: همی + کند is the classical epic progressive: همی marks repeated/ongoing action and کند is the verb 'do/make' in the narrative. The subject سودابه follows the verb (verb–subject order, common in Ferdowsi), and از خشم موی is a prepositional phrase (from/ because of anger) plus the object موی (hair) — the whole segment means 'Sudabeh kept pulling/tearing her hair in anger.'
segments.s160-g546: Again همی marks repeated action. ریخت with آب normally means 'poured water' (here a repeated/past narrative action — ریخت is the simple past form used with همی for continuous/repeated behavior). و links to a second coordinated clause; خست is an archaic/ambiguous form attached to روی (face), so the clause pattern is parallel: 'she kept pouring water and kept [verb] her face.'
segments.s160-g547: چو here is a temporal conjunction 'when'; پیش پدر is a locative/phrasing 'in the presence of his father'. شد is the narrative past (here conveying 'came/appeared'), and the subject سیاووش follows the verb (verb–subject order). پاک is a predicate adjective applied to Siawush: 'Siawush was pure.'
segments.s160-g548: This is a series of negated nouns introduced by repeated نه ... نه (neither ... nor). The connectors و are simple coordinators. The line lists what is absent around/with him: دود (smoke), آتش (fire), گرد (here 'dust'), خاک (earth/dirt).
segments.s160-g549: فرود آمد is a compound verbal phrase meaning 'alighted' or 'came down' (فرود = descent, آمد = came). از اسپ is a prepositional phrase 'from the horse/steed', and the subject کاووس شاه follows the verb in the typical epic V–S order: 'Kavus the king dismounted from his steed.'
segments.s160-g550: This is a verbless/elliptical listing: پیاده سپهبد پیاده سپاه. پیاده 'on foot' qualifies the following nouns. The omitted verb (understood from context) is something like 'was' or 'came' — so: 'the spahbed on foot, the army on foot.' Repetition and brevity are typical of the epic register.
segments.s160-g551: سیاووش را uses را as a definite/direct-object/topic marker. تنگ در برگرفت is a verbal phrase where در برگرفت (literally 'took in') with تنگ 'tight' conveys being closely enclosed or seized — verb برگرفت is past narrative 'seized/embraced/surrounded'. Word order places the object before the verb+modifier (typical in the epic style).
segments.s160-g552: ز here functions as 'because of' (poetic variant of از). کردار بد is a genitive compound 'bad deed/behavior'. پوزش اندر گرفت is an idiomatic verbal phrase: گرفت (past 'took') with اندر (in/into) indicates 'took upon himself / assumed' (here, 'he sought or offered pardon'). The clause thus says that on account of the bad deed he took up/asked for pardon.
segments.s160-g561: به + noun + verb: به ایوان is a prepositional phrase 'to/toward the iwan', followed by the past-tense verb خرامید ('he strode/proudly walked') — an older past form. و links to the next clause; بنشست is past third-person 'he sat down'. شاد is a postverbal predicate adjective modifying his manner ('joyful').
segments.s160-g562: کلاه کیانی is a noun (کلاه) followed by a nisba/adjectival form (کیانی = Kayanian/royal), i.e. 'the Kayanian cap'. به سر is a locative prepositional phrase literally 'to/onto the head'. برنهاد is an older past form of نهادن with the prefix بر- meaning 'placed on' or 'set upon'.
segments.s160-g563: می‌آورد is the imperfect/habitual form with the present prefix می- used here as a narrative/actional present ('he brings' / 'he brought'). و connects clauses. رامشگران is the plural noun 'comforters/peacemakers'; را is the definite-object marker marking رامشگران as the object. بخواند is an older past/simple third-person form of خواندن here meaning 'called/summoned'.
segments.s160-g564: همه کامها is the noun phrase 'all desires/wishes' (همه = 'all', کامها = plural 'wishes/desires'). با + name (با سیاوش) indicates accompaniment/goal 'with/for Siavash'. براند is the older third-person verb راندن ('to drive, to direct'); here it means 'he set/drove (the delights) toward Siavash' — i.e. 'he gave/granted all delights to Siavash.'
segments.s160-g565: سه روز is a temporal phrase 'three days'. اندر آن سور is a locative phrase 'in/at that banquet' (اندر = 'in/within', آن = 'that', سور = 'feast/banquet'). می‌در کشید is a poetic/compound expression meaning 'it continued/was prolonged' — می- marks ongoing/habitual aspect and کشید here conveys 'stretched/lasted' (a poetic use).
segments.s160-g566: نبد is an archaic negative/existential 'there was not / did not exist'. بر در is a locative string 'on the door' (بر = 'on/upon', در = here 'door/gate'). گنج بند و کلید lists the items 'treasure-house (or vault) + بند (a fastening/lock) and کلید (key)'; syntactically نبد negates their existence on the door.
segments.s160-g567: چهارم is an ordinal 'the fourth (day)'. به تخت کیی is a prepositional phrase 'onto the Kayanian throne' (تخت = 'throne', کیی = 'Kayanian/royal' as a descriptive adjective). برنشست is a past third-person form meaning 'he sat upon' or 'took his seat on'.
segments.s160-g568: یکی here functions as an indefinite numeral 'a certain/one' introducing a single item. گرزه is the noun 'mace' (weapon). گاو پیکر is a compound adjectival phrase 'cow-bodied' i.e. 'shaped like a bull' (گاو = 'cow/bull', پیکر = 'figure/shape'). به دست is a locative phrase 'in (his) hand' (به + دست). The whole noun phrase describes the mace he held.
segments.s160-g521: Literal word order is subject/agent omitted — 'جهانی نهاده به کاووس چشم' is a compact poetic clause: 'جهانی' (lit. 'a world') is the direct object/thing placed, 'نهاده' is a past participle/verb meaning 'placed/put', and 'به کاووس چشم' is a prepositional phrase 'before/in Kavus's sight' (به + name + noun). The phrase therefore means 'placed a world before Kavus's eyes.'
segments.s160-g522: This is two parallel nominal phrases joined by و. 'زبان پر ز دشنام' = 'tongue full of insults' uses the adjective/particle 'پر' (full of) followed by 'ز' + noun ('دشنام'); the same pattern repeats for 'دل پر ز خشم' = 'heart full of anger'. The nouns 'زبان' and 'دل' are the topics, and 'پر ز ...' functions as their complement.
segments.s160-g523: Standard SOV/PV order: proper name 'سیاوش' (subject) + object 'سیه' marked by the object marker 'را', then adverbial 'به تندی' ('with haste') and the past verb 'بتاخت' (archaic past 'he charged/rode swiftly'). 'بتاخت' is Ferdowsi's poetic past-tense verb for charging/galloping.
segments.s160-g524: This line is compressed/elliptical. 'نشد' is past negative 'did not become/happen'; followed by the predicate adjective 'تنگدل' ('faint/narrow‑hearted') describing someone (or some people) who 'did not' do something. The sequence 'جنگ آتش بساخت' reads as a separate clause: 'جنگ' (the battle/war) is the subject and 'آتش بساخت' describes its effect — 'kindled/produced fire' (poetic phrasing). The verb-form 'بساخت' here is irregular in the supplied senses and likely a poetic/archaic build/make — no matching sense provided, so marked -1.
segments.s160-g525: 'ز هر سو' is a prepositional phrase 'from every side/direction.' The noun 'زبانه' (plural implied by sense) 'tongues [of flame]' is the subject, 'همی' is the old/poetic progressive particle indicating ongoing action, and 'برکشید' is the past verb 'lifted up/rose.' Together: 'Flames rose up from every side.'
segments.s160-g526: 'کسی ... ندید' is a negative existential: here 'کسی' = 'no one' (contextual negative), 'خود' is an emphatic reflexive 'himself', 'و' connects, and 'اسپ سیاوش' is 'Siavash's horse/steed' (noun + possessor). 'ندید' is the past negative 'did not see.' So: 'No one saw him or Siavash's horse.'
segments.s160-g527: 'یکی' here is the indefinite 'a certain' (a man/figure), 'دشت' names the setting 'plain', and the phrase 'با دیدگان پر ز خون' is a descriptive prepositional phrase 'with eyes full of blood' — 'با' + instrument/attribute + 'دیدگان' (eyes) + 'پر ز' (full of) + 'خون' (blood).
segments.s160-g528: This is an indirect interrogative/temporal clause: 'که' introduces the clause, 'تا' here works with the question word 'کی' to mark 'until/when', 'او' is the third‑person pronoun 'he', and 'آید' is the present/future‑oriented verb 'will come' (standard poetic present). 'ز آتش برون' is the prepositional complement 'out of the fire.' Together: '... wondering when he would come out of the fire.'
segments.s160-g641: برین is the fused preposition بر + این (ʻupon thisʼ) and introduces a topical phrase; داستان is its object (ʻthis taleʼ). زد is the verbal nucleus (literally ʻstruckʼ) used here metaphorically to mean ʻintervened/utteredʼ, and یکی رهنمون follows as the actor (ʻa certain guideʼ). The order (prepositional phrase + verb + subject) is a poetic inversion: the prepositional phrase sets the scene, زد gives the action, and یکی رهنمون names who acted.
segments.s160-g642: که introduces reported speech or the content of what was said (ʻthat/whichʼ). مهری فزون نیست is predicative: مهری (a love/measure of affection) + فزون (ʻmore/greaterʼ) + نیست (ʻis notʼ) → ʻthere is no love greaterʼ. از here marks the comparison (ʻthanʼ), and مهر خون is a genitive compound ʻlove of bloodʼ (i.e. blood‑lust or love tied to blood). The whole clause therefore states the propositional content of the guide's remark.
segments.s160-g643: چو is the temporal conjunction ʻwhenʼ. فرزند شایسته is a noun phrase ʻa worthy child/sonʼ (modifier after noun is typical in Persian poetry), and آمد پدید uses two verbs in sequence (آمد ʻcameʼ + پدید ʻappeared/manifestedʼ) — a common poetic doubling meaning ʻcame into being/appearedʼ.
segments.s160-g644: ز is the older/poetic form of از (ʻfrom/away fromʼ); مهر زنان = ʻthe love/affection for womenʼ (مهر + women as a compound). دل is the affected organ ʻthe heartʼ. بباید is the modal ʻmust/ought toʼ and is followed by برید (here ʻto cut/severʼ) to yield an impersonal obligation ʻthe heart must be severedʼ (i.e. one must give up attachment). Word order places the source of attachment first (ʻfrom the love of womenʼ) and then states the required action.
segments.s160-g529: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'; the clause follows with object-marked pronoun او را and the past plural verb بدیدند ('they saw'). برخاست غو is a poetic inversion where the verb برخاست ('arose') comes before its noun غو ('uproar'), yielding 'an uproar arose.'
segments.s160-g530: که introduces a content clause ('that/for'), giving the reason or report. آمد is simple past 'came'; the source phrase ز آتش ('from the fire') precedes the directional adverb برون ('out/forth') in this elevated word order. شاه نو is the noun + postpositive adjective pattern meaning 'the new king.'
segments.s160-g531: اگر begins a conditional ('if'). بودی is the past form 'you were' (2nd-person past) used in the protasis. مگر‌تر is a comparative/qualifying particle here ('more, even more') attached to -تر; شدی is the past 2nd-person of شدن ('you became'), giving a counterfactual/result clause governed by the condition.
segments.s160-g532: ز تری is an ablative phrase 'from/through wetness' that sets the cause. همه جامه is a nominal phrase 'all (the) garments' and the verb شدی (2nd-person past) follows without an explicit pronoun—classical Persian often omits the subject; here شدی says 'you/they became' (result state). بی‌بر is a predicate adjective meaning 'bereft/ill‑fated' applied to the garments or the person’s state.
segments.s160-g533: چنان is an adverb 'so/thus' modifying آمد (past 'came'). The nouns اسپ و قبای سوار are coordinated: اسپ ('steed/horse') and قبای سوار ('the rider's robe' or 'the riderly cloak'); سوار follows قبای as a postpositive modifier (common in classical Persian).
segments.s160-g534: که introduces a descriptive/result clause ('such that'). گفتی here is an archaic/poetic impersonal or evidential use related to گفتن, meaning roughly 'one would say' or 'it seemed' (not the conversational noun listed in the lemma). سمن داشت اندر کنار is straightforward: سمن 'jasmine' as the thing, داشت 'had' (past) and اندر کنار 'at his side/nearby.'
segments.s160-g535: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when.' بخشایش پاک یزدان is a noun phrase with noun + adjective + divine name in apposition: 'the pure forgiveness/mercy of God.' بود is the past (3rd person singular) 'was,' completing the temporal clause.
segments.s160-g536: دم آتش و آب is a coordinated noun phrase 'the breath/force of fire and (of) water' (دم used in the sense 'breath/force'). یکسان بود is the predicate 'were equal' (یکسان 'equal' + بود 'was' — classical Persian often uses a singular copula after a predicate adjective that applies to a compound subject).
segments.s160-g569: برآشفت is a past, intransitive poetic verb ‘to become angry’ (verb precedes the coordinating و). سودابه را پیش خواند is a transitive clause: سودابه + را (definite object marker) appears before the verb خواند. پیش here is a locative/adverbial ‘before/in the presence of’, and خواند (past) means ‘called/summoned’ rather than ‘read’. The sequence gives two coordinated actions: he became angry and he summoned Sudabeh.
segments.s160-g570: گذشت سخنها is a short clause meaning ‘the words passed’ or ‘when the talk was over’; note the poetic verb-first order. برو is an imperative ‘go!’ addressing someone. بر براند is a verbal instruction where براند (from راندن) means ‘drive away’; the preceding بر is here a prefix/prepositional element forming the sense ‘drive away/away from’, so the two imperatives together amount to ‘Go, drive her away.’
segments.s160-g571: که introduces a content clause (‘that/for’). بی‌شرمی و بد are coordinated nouns/adjectives ‘shamelessness and evil/badness’. بسی here is an emphatic/adverbial older-use meaning ‘very/much’, and کرده‌ای is the perfect/past form ‘you have done/committed’ (2nd person singular perfect), so the whole clause says ‘that you have done much shameless and bad [deeds]’.
segments.s160-g572: فراوان is an adverb here modifying the following predicate (‘greatly’). دل من is the possessed noun phrase ‘my heart’ (noun before the possessor in this poetic order). بیازرده‌ای is a past perfect (2nd person) form built on آزردن meaning ‘to hurt/offend’ and here functions ‘you have wounded/hurt’, so the clause reads ‘you have greatly wounded my heart.’
segments.s160-g573: یکی here is an indefinite/emphatic ‘one’ or ‘a certain’ (used to single out the act). بد نمودی is past of نمودن ‘to show/appear’, meaning ‘you showed/acted badly’. به فرجام کار is a prepositional phrase ‘at/to the outcome of the affair’ (به + noun phrase), so the whole clause means ‘you made/showed a bad thing at the affair’s outcome.’
segments.s160-g574: که again introduces the following admonition/explanation. بر جان is a prepositional phrase ‘on/upon the life (of)’, فرزند من is the possessed noun phrase ‘my child/son’, and زینهار is an interjection/imperative ‘take heed!/beware!’. The sequence is an urgent admonition concerning the child’s life.
segments.s160-g575: بخوردی is a past (2nd sing.) verb meaning here ‘you struck/it befell by your action’ (contextually ‘you struck/caused harm’). The conjunction و links actions. در آتش is the locative phrase ‘into/in the fire’ and انداختی is the transitive past ‘you threw/cast (him)’, with the direct object omitted but understood from context. Together: ‘you struck and cast (him) into the fire.’
segments.s160-g576: برین گونه is an archaic/poetic locution ‘in this manner/way’ (بر + این/ین + گونه). بر جادویی is a small noun phrase where جادویی ‘magical/sorcerous’ is the object/description and the preceding بر gives the sense ‘in the matter of / as (a)’, and ساختی is the past ‘you made/did’. The clause says ‘by this means you made (it) into sorcery’ — i.e., ‘you wrought magic in this way.’
segments.s160-g137: بسازم‌گر is the verb-plus-conditional particle: بسازم (1st‑person present/subjunctive of ساختن) + گر (‘if’). It gives a conditional 'if I reconcile / if I make peace'. او سربپیچد follows as a subordinate clause (سربپیچد is 3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive/optative meaning 'turn aside/turn his head'), and ز من attaches as a simple prepositional phrase 'from me / away from me'.
segments.s160-g138: کنم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive of کردن used here with a noun to mean 'I will make/utter' (literally 'I do/make'). The sequence ز + (او) is the poetic preposition meaning 'because of / on account of / against him' (here 'because of him/against him'); the annotation splits that into ز and و, so the explanation treats them together. فغان is a noun 'lament/cry' used with کردن to form 'to cry out'. بر سر انجمن is an idiomatic locative phrase 'before/upon the head of the assembly' = 'before the assembly', with بر = on/upon, سر = head, انجمن = assembly.
segments.s160-g139: نشست here is the past/telic verb 'he sat' (classical past form). از بر تخت is a classical locative expression meaning 'on/at the throne' (از بر is an older prepositional combination used where Modern Persian would use بر or بر روی). باگوشوار is a adjectival phrase 'with earring(s)' modifying the person who sat.
segments.s160-g140: This is a verb‑object phrase in which نهاد is the past form of نهادن 'placed/put' and the sequence به سر بر نهاد literally orders the datives—به سر (onto the head) + بر نهاد (placed upon). افسری پرنگار is a noun phrase 'an / the افسر (diadem/helmet) richly ornamented' where پرنگار (lit. 'full of design') postmodifies افسری.
segments.s160-g141: سیاوخش را uses the definite/object marker را to mark the direct object. در بر خویش is a fixed classical phrase meaning 'into/at his own side / to his presence' (خویش = 'his own', here formed from خوی + ش). خواند is the past tense of خواندن used transitively as 'he called/summoned'. Word order is object + locative phrase + verb, typical of classical Persian inversion.
segments.s160-g142: ز هر گونه is a prepositional phrase 'of/from every sort/kind' (ز used poetically = 'of/regarding'). با او 'with him' marks the interlocutor; سخنها is the plural noun 'speeches/matters/topics'. براند is 3rd‑person singular present of راندن (here used figuratively) — in context it means 'uttered/put forth / discussed' rather than a literal 'drive away', so the clause means 'he put forth / spoke many topics with him.'
segments.s160-g143: بدو گفت is the classical 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him', گفت = 'said' / here represented by the nominal lemma but functioning verbally). The following verbless clause گنجی بیاراست شاه places the verb بیاراست (past of بی‌آراستن, 'has prepared/adorned') before the subject شاه (inverted order): 'the king has prepared/adorned a treasure.'
segments.s160-g144: This is a relative/comparative clause introduced by کزان سان 'from/of such a sort' (= 'such that'). ندیدست is a perfect‑form meaning 'has not been seen' (impersonal/predicative), کس is the indefinite 'anyone' (here 'no one has seen'), and تاج و گاه are the coordinated nouns 'crown and throne/occasion' describing what has not been seen — i.e. 'a crown and throne like this has not been seen by anyone.' Classical word order places the verb before its logical subject (کس).
segments.s160-g473: ز دور از دو فرسنگ is a poetic double prepositional phrase meaning 'from afar, some two farsangs away' (ز ... از ... gives layered 'from' sense). هرکش is an indefinite 'whoever' (هر کس in older spelling), and بدید is the past tense 'he saw' (third-person past). Word order places the location phrase up front for emphasis.
segments.s160-g474: چنین modifies what follows ('thus'). جست is the past of 'to leap/spring' (he leapt), و is the coordinating 'and', while جوی here is the noun 'search'/'seeking' (so the phrase reads like 'he leapt and sought'). بلا را is the definite object ('the calamity' with را marking it), and کلید is figurative 'key' — 'the key to the calamity.'
segments.s160-g475: همی is a classical emphatic/continuous particle here with a past verb (marks ongoing or intent: 'he was wanting/endeavoured'); خواست is past 'he wanted' and دیدن is the infinitive 'to see'. در راستی is a prepositional phrase 'in truth' or 'into the truth of the matter.'
segments.s160-g476: ز marks source 'from' (poetic). کار here means 'deed/act', and زن is 'the woman' (agent). آید is present/past-present 'comes/comes about' (used generically), همه = 'all/every', and کاستی = 'deficiency/shortcoming' — together: 'from the woman's deed comes every shortcoming.'
segments.s160-g477: چو here functions as a conditional 'if'. این داستان is 'this story/tale'. سر به سر is an idiom 'from beginning to end' / 'through and through' (literally 'head to head'), and بشنوی is the present-subjunctive/imperative form 'you hear' / 'you are told' (classical use meaning 'you listen to').
segments.s160-g478: به آید literally 'it will come' or 'you will be moved/led' (آید used impersonally). ترا‌گر is a fused conditional 'if to you' / 'should it happen to you'. بدین is به + دین = 'to this (religion/creed)', and بگروی is the subjunctive 'you adopt/accept' (گرویدن = to take on a belief). Together: 'you may come, if you accept this creed' — i.e. 'you may be led to embrace this faith.'
segments.s160-g479: نهادند is the third-person plural past of نهادن 'they placed/they set'. بر دشت is the locative 'on the plain'. هیزم دو کوه is a poetic noun phrase 'two mountains (heaps) of firewood' — هیزم ('wood/kindling') modifying 'دو کوه' (two heaps/mountains).
segments.s160-g480: جهانی here means 'the whole world' or 'everyone' (universal). نظاره is 'view/looking' (a noun), شده is a participle/past marker ('was / had become' — here forming a passive-like or resultant state: 'was watched / looked on'), and هم‌گروه functions adverbially 'together / assembled' (literally 'of the same group'). The line thus says that all the world watched, gathered together.
segments.s160-g577: نیاید is an impersonal negative verb meaning 'it does not befit / is not proper'; ترا is the 2nd-person enclitic (to you); پوزش is a noun 'apology/pardon'; اکنون 'now'; به + کار = 'in / with regard to the affair/task'. Word order is poetic: the verb 'نیاید' comes first to emphasize that an apology is inappropriate for you at this moment regarding this matter.
segments.s160-g578: بپرداز is an imperative 'attend to / deal with'; جای 'place/position' here means the location or post to be looked after; و 'and/then' links two imperatives; برآرای is an imperative of برآراستن 'arrange/adorn/put in order' and governs کار 'work/task'. Together: two commands — tend the place and set the work in order.
segments.s160-g579: نشاید is an impersonal modal 'it is not fitting/appropriate'; که introduces the subordinate clause; باشی is subjunctive/present-form of بودن 'to be' used here in the sense 'that you should be / remain'; تو is the pronoun 'you' (emphatic/subject); اندر زمین 'on/in the earth' meaning 'alive in the world' or simply 'on the earth'.
segments.s160-g580: جز introduces the only exception 'except'; آویختن is a verbal noun 'hanging' (gerund) used as the sole possible fate; نیست + پاداش این literally 'there is no reward/compensation for this' — word order places the exception first for emphasis: 'there is no recompense for this except hanging.'
segments.s160-g581: بدو 'to him/unto him' (dative); گفت here functions as the past of گفتن 'said' (the provided sense list did not include 'said' so it is a direct past-tense reporting verb); سودابه is the speaker's name; کای شهریار is a vocative address 'O king' (کای = 'O', شهریار = 'king'). Word order is 'to him said Sūdābeh, “O king…”' as typical in epic narration.
segments.s160-g582: تو is the vocative/imperative addressee 'you'; آتش 'fire' is the direct object; the cluster written ب + دین here functions as part of a verbal phrase equivalent to 'upon this' / 'on this one' (in the manuscript it appears as بدین), introducing the target of the action; تارک من literally 'my leaver/one who has abandoned me' (tārak used as a noun = 'the one who left me'); ببار is an imperative 'pour' or 'let fall' (of fire). Overall: an imperative request to the king to hurl/fire upon the person who abandoned her.
segments.s160-g583: مرا‌گر is a conditional particle meaning 'if it were to me / if it were the case for me'; همی here is an emphatic adverb 'indeed/just' (used in older poetic register); سر 'head' is the object; بباید 'it must / ought' expresses necessity; برید is the verbal stem appearing after the modal 'بباید' and here means 'to be cut off' (the provided senses do not include this sense, so the correct sense is not listed). The clause expresses the hypothetical 'if my head must be cut off...'.
segments.s160-g584: مکافات 'retribution/punishment' (subject); این 'this' (the foregoing); بد 'bad/evil' qualifies مکافات; که introduces a relative/nominal clause 'that'; بر من 'upon me' (بر + من), with من here the object pronoun 'me'; رسید is perfect 'arrived/reached' used idiomatically 'befell me'. The whole segment: 'this bad punishment that has befallen me.'
segments.s160-g457: کزین = archaic compound equivalent to modern از این, here meaning 'of/from these'; یکی‌گر is a conditional form (یکی + گر, گر = 'if') meaning 'if one (of them)'; شود is the classical present/subjunctive of شدن 'become'; نابکار is a predicate adjective 'wicked/evil'. Word order is inverted/compact as in epic verse: 'if one of these becomes wicked.'
segments.s160-g458: ازان پس که is a temporal connective 'thereupon/after that, when'; خواند is a past tense verb here 'called/summoned' (the king summoned); م + را are written separately in the gloss but together form the object 'mara' = 'me' (م is the clitic 'me' and را is the definite object marker); شهریار is the subject 'the king' placed after the verb for poetic word-order. The clause therefore reads 'after that when the king called me.'
segments.s160-g459: چو introduces a hypothetical/conditional 'if' here; فرزند و زن are coordinated nouns 'child and wife'; باشدم is an archaic/poetic first-person form indicating a hypothetical 'were I / if I had' (the clitic/subject 'I' is implied); خون و مغز are the nouns 'blood and brain' placed after the hypothetical clause to indicate what would be affected. The clause is elliptical as is typical in verse.
segments.s160-g460: کرا is an archaic relative/pronominal 'whose' or 'who/whom' referring back to the persons mentioned; بیش modifies the following verb phrase as 'more' or 'to a greater degree'; بیرون شود is a verbal phrase 'comes out / is spilled' (بیرون + شدن); کار نغز is a nominal phrase 'a pleasant/fitting affair' with the adjective نغز postposed. Together: 'whose blood and brains were more spilled, the affair would be pleasant.'
segments.s160-g461: همان به is a demonstrative/particle cluster 'that very / therefore' or 'exactly so'; کزین = 'from this / because of this'; زشت‌کردار is a compound noun 'ill‑doer, misbehaver' modifying the following noun; دل is the noun 'heart' and is the object/theme that the speaker will act upon in the next segment. The phrase sets up the reason: 'for this ill‑doer, (regarding) the heart...'.
segments.s160-g462: بشویم here functions as a verbal form meaning 'I will wash/cleanse' (classical poetic form; the corpus gloss lists a different lemma, so the exact lemma senses don't match modern listings); کنم is the first‑person singular of کردن 'I will make/do' used to introduce the following object; چاره is 'remedy/solution'; دلگسل is an adjectival compound 'heart‑rending / heart‑breaking' describing the kind of remedy. So the clause reads 'I will cleanse (my) heart and make a remedy for the heart‑breaking.'
segments.s160-g463: چه گفت is an interrogative phrase 'what did (he) say?' (چه = what; گفت = said). In poetic word order the verb precedes the subject; آن سپهدار نیکوسخن is 'that commander, eloquent of speech' (نیکوسخن postmodifies سپهدار). The whole is 'What did that eloquent commander say?'
segments.s160-g464: که introduces the reported clause 'that/which'; با بددلی = 'with ill will/malice' (با = with, بددلی = ill will); شهریاری is the noun 'sovereignty / rulership' and here names the action/role; مکن is the negative imperative 'do not' (or negative present subjunctive) placed at the end to forbid the action. Together: 'that (you) do not rule with ill will.'
segments.s160-g537: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning 'when' introducing a subordinate clause. The phrase follows classical Persian word order with the verb گذشت (past 3sg) at the end; کوه آتش is a poetic noun phrase 'mountain (of) fire' where آتش qualifies کوه without an ezāfe. به هامون marks direction 'into the plain.'
segments.s160-g538: خروشیدن is a verbal noun/infinitive 'roaring' used as the nominal subject or event, followed by آمد (past 3sg 'came') forming the predicate 'a roar came up.' ز شهر و ز دشت is an ablative/source phrase 'from the city and from the plain.'
segments.s160-g539: سواران لشکر is a compound nominal phrase meaning 'the army's horsemen' or 'the mounted troops of the host' (poetic apposition without ezāfe). برانگیختند is past plural 'they rose/sprang up' agreeing with the plural subject and indicates sudden arousal or movement.
segments.s160-g540: همه دشت is a collective expression 'the whole plain' (i.e., everyone across the plain). پیشش 'in front of him / in his presence' is an adverbial phrase; درم is the direct object 'dirhams' and ریختند is past plural 'they poured/threw' — here idiomatically 'they showered coins' before him.
segments.s160-g541: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain' placed before the noun شادمانی 'rejoicing/joy.' The adjective بد follows the noun (classical Persian order) giving 'an ill/evil rejoicing.' اندر جهان is a locative phrase 'in the world.'
segments.s160-g542: میان ... و میان ... repeats the preposition 'among/between' for rhetorical emphasis. کهان and مهان are collective/poetic nouns; the phrase means 'among the (ancient) ones and among the visitors/guests' with the parallel structure placing the groups in contrast or coordination.
segments.s160-g543: همی is an adverb indicating continuous or repeated action ('kept/continued to'). داد is the verb 'gave' (past), and مژده is the object 'good news.' یکی را دگر is an idiomatic sequence 'one (news) then another,' with را marking the object (یکی).
segments.s160-g544: که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. بخشود is past 3sg 'forgave/granted remission.' بر بیگنه is a prepositional phrase 'to/upon the stranger' (بر used here as 'to'), and دادگر 'the just one' functions as the agent/subject placed after the verb for poetic word-order emphasis: 'the just ruler pardoned the stranger.'
segments.s160-g593: This is an instance of inverted word order common in poetic Persian: the indirect object phrase 'به ایرانیان' (to the Iranians) comes first, followed by the past-tense verb 'گفت' (said), and then the subject 'شاه جهان' (the king of the world). 'گفت' is 3rd singular past and functions here as the main speech verb introducing the subordinate clause that follows.
segments.s160-g594: 'کزین' is a contracted/poetic form meaning 'from/for this' (literally 'که از این'), introducing cause or reference. 'بد' is a noun here meaning 'evil' or 'ill deed.' The conjunction/relative 'که' links to the following clause 'این ساخت اندر نهان' ('this he made in secret'), where 'ساخت' is past tense 'he made/did' and 'اندر نهان' is a locative phrase 'in secret' placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s160-g595: 'چه سازم' is a fixed rhetorical/exclamatory phrase meaning 'what shall I do?' where 'سازم' is 1st person singular present/subjunctive of ساخت/ساز ('to make/do'). The following clause 'چه باشد مکافات این' is another exclamation: 'چه باشد' ('what will be') uses 'باشد' in an optative/subjunctive sense expressing uncertainty or wonder, governing the noun phrase 'مکافات این' ('the recompense of this'). Word order places the noun after 'باشد' for poetic effect.
segments.s160-g596: 'همه' here means 'everyone' (the gathered people). 'شاه را' is the marked object/target of the acclamation (the preposition-like object marker 'را' highlights 'the king' as the recipient). 'خواندند' is past plural 'they chanted/uttered' and takes 'آفرین' (an acclamation) as its complement; together it means 'they cried out ‘‘Bravo!’’ to the king.'
segments.s160-g597: The subordinating 'که' introduces the content of the proclamation: 'پاداش این' means 'the recompense for this.' 'آنکه' is a relative phrase 'that he (who)...' and 'بیجان شود' literally 'becomes lifeless' uses 'شود' as a present/subjunctive of 'شد' expressing what will befall him ('shall be put to death' or 'become lifeless').
segments.s160-g598: 'ز' (poetic for 'از') means 'because of' or 'from.' 'بد کردن' is a verbal noun phrase 'doing evil' ('بد' + light verb 'کردن'). 'خوی' + enclitic 'ش' together form 'خویش' (his own disposition), here written split as 'خوی' and 'ش.' 'پیچان شود' is a predicate: 'پیچان' (twisted/deceptive) with 'شود' (become) — 'his disposition will become twisted/deceitful' as a consequence of his evil acts.
segments.s160-g599: 'به دژخیم فرمود' is 'he ordered/commanded the torturer' — 'به' introduces the indirect object, 'دژخیم' is the addressee, and 'فرمود' is a polite/royal past-tense verb meaning 'commanded/said.' 'کاین را' (that this one) is the object of the order; 'به کوی' specifies the place 'into the street/quarter.' Note the poetic fused relative 'کاین' = 'که این' ('that this').
segments.s160-g600: 'ز دار اندر آویز' literally 'hang (him) from the gallows' — 'ز' = 'from/of', 'دار' here is 'gallows' (poetic use), 'اندر' = 'in/on', and 'آویز' functions as an imperative/infinitival command 'hang.' The conjunction 'و' links to 'برتاب روی': 'برتاب' is another imperative 'bear/endure/show' directed to the torturer and 'روی' primarily means 'face' — together the phrase is an order to display or tolerate his face before the people (a formula for public exposure). Both imperatives are terse, as typical in commands.
segments.s160-g505: رخ شاه کاووس = “the face of King Kavus”: Persian places the possessed noun (رخ) before its possessor (شاه کاووس). پر شرم is a predicative phrase meaning “full of shame” (پر = “full (of)”; followed by the noun that fills it). دید is the past-tense verb “saw/beheld” (3rd person) that closes the clause; the subject is implicit in the narrative (“he saw”).
segments.s160-g506: سخن گفتنش is a nominalized verb phrase: سخن “speech” + گفتنش (گفتن + enclitic -ش) = “his speaking / his speech” or “the way he spoke.” با پسر marks the companion “with the son/young man.” نرم is an adjectival predicate modifying the speech (“gentle”); the final دید again is the past verb “he saw,” applying to this whole descriptive clause.
segments.s160-g507: سیاوش بدو گفت = “Siyavash said to him”: بدو is the dative/enclitic sense “to him.” گفت here introduces direct speech and is the past-tense verb “said.” انده مدار is an admonition meaning roughly “do not grieve / be distressed” (a concise, poetic prohibition); it functions as the quoted imperative following گفت.
segments.s160-g508: کزین سان = “from this / in this manner” (prepositional phrase indicating cause or manner). بود is the past copula “was.” گردش روزگار is a nominal phrase “the turning (گردش) of the times/the world (روزگار)” — together idiomatically meaning the wheel/turning of fortune or how the world turns; the clause states that the way things happen is as just described.
segments.s160-g509: سر پر ز شرم و بهایی = “my head full of shame and (also) of worth/price”: سر (head) is the noun, پر + ز + [noun] = “full of [noun],” and و links شرم and بهایی. مراست here is a poetic/copular ending meaning roughly “is mine / belongs to me” or a copula asserting the state; the clause thus asserts the speaker’s condition (head full of shame and value).
segments.s160-g510: اگر بیگناهم = “if I am innocent”: اگر introduces a conditional; بیگناهم is the adjectival/predicative form with the 1st-person enclitic -م (“I am innocent”). رهایی مراست = “release/freedom is mine”: رهایی is the noun “release/freedom,” and مراست repeats the copular/possessive ending indicating that this release pertains to the speaker.
segments.s160-g511: ور ایدونک = a conditional/ concessive opener meaning roughly “but if / even if (even now).” زین کار = “of this deed” (زین = “this/of this,” کار = “deed/act”). هستم گناه = an inversion where هستم (I am) combines with گناه (guilt/sin) to yield “I am guilty” or “there is guilt on me”; Persian poetic order often places the predicate noun after the copula.
segments.s160-g512: جهان آفرینم is a compound referring to the world-creator (i.e., God); the phrase functions as the subject. ندارد is the present 3rd-person negative of داشتن “to have” and here idiomatically means “does not give/does not have.” نگاه “a look / regard” is the object — together meaning “the Creator of the world does not give a glance/has no regard” (i.e., will not look upon or show mercy).
segments.s160-g633: چنان چون ... بود is a subordinating/time clause meaning 'when/inasmuch as it was so that'. بود is the past copula 'was'. مردم ترسکار is a nominal phrase 'the people (were) fearful/cowardly' with ترسکار qualifying مردم.
segments.s160-g634: برآید is an intransitive verb 'come about/arise' placed before its complements (typical poetic order). به کام دل is a prepositional phrase 'to/for the satisfaction (کام) of the heart (دل)', and مرد کار is a genitive construction 'the man's affair/work' that is the thing which 'comes about' to the heart's desire.
segments.s160-g635: بجایی که is a subordinating phrase meaning 'in a case / at a time when'. زهر آگند is a compound adjectival notion 'poison‑filled / tainted with poison' that qualifies روزگار ('the times' or 'the age'). Word order places the modifier before روزگار.
segments.s160-g636: ازو is a prepositional/genitive 'from it/of it'. نوش is the noun 'drink/draught' (object), and خیره مکن is an imperative negative 'do not [do X]' with خیره functioning adverbially here (classical usage implying excess/over‑eagerness) and مکن 'do not'. خواستار is a vocative/epithet 'seeker/desirous one' addressing the addressee.
segments.s160-g637: تو is the address 'you'. با آفرینش بسنده is a predicative phrase 'sufficient/content with creation' where بسنده functions as predicate/adverbial 'content/sufficient'. نه‌ای is the archaic negative copula 'you are not' (equivalent to modern تو نیستی).
segments.s160-g638: مشو is the negative imperative 'do not become'. تیز‌گر is a noun 'sharpener' (used metaphorically: one who hones or makes keen). پرورنده نه‌ای is a following clause 'you are not the nurturer' (پرورنده as predicate), so the speaker forbids adopting the role of a 'sharpener' because 'you are not a nurturer'.
segments.s160-g639: چنین‌ست is a fixed formula 'thus it is / so'. کردار گردان سپهر is a genitive/nominal string 'the action/way (کردار) of the revolving (گردان) sky/heavens (سپهر)', with گردان qualifying سپهر and کردار governing the phrase.
segments.s160-g640: نخواهد گشادن is a negative-future construction 'will not open/show' (نخواهد = 'will not/won't'), with گشادن 'to open/reveal'. همی is an emphatic/adverbial particle often meaning 'ever/always' here giving the sense 'will not ever'. بر تو چهر = 'its face toward you' (بر + تو + چهر), so the whole clause says the heavens will not ever show their face to you.
segments.s160-g625: دگر باره is an adverbial phrase meaning "again; once more" (دگر + باره). The following prepositional phrase با شهریار جهان places the company/agent after the preposition با (with) in normal Persian order: "with the king of the world."
segments.s160-g626: همی before a past-form verb marks continuous or iterative action in epic/classical Persian ("kept on / was continually"). جادوی is the noun "magic/sorcery" as the thing acted upon and ساخت is the past of ساختن "made/did". اندر نهان is a fixed adverbial phrase "in secret / secretly" (اندر = in, نهان = hidden/secretly).
segments.s160-g627: بدان here is a compounded adverbial sense (ب + دان) meaning "thereby / by that" or "with that"; تا introduces a purpose/result clause "so that," taking شود (subjunctive/optative of شدن) to mean "may happen / might become." با سیاووش بد means "ill/harm with Siavash" = "that ill may befall Siavash" (با = with/to affect, سیاووش = the prince, بد used adverbially = badly/ill).
segments.s160-g628: بدانسان is an adverb meaning "in that way / thus." که introduces the relative/explanatory clause "which/that," از here means "from/of," گوهر denotes "essence/nature," او is the third‑person pronoun "his," and سزد is the impersonal/present form "it befits / it is fitting" — "in such a way as befits his essence."
segments.s160-g629: ز is the poetic form of از meaning "from / because of." گفتار او = "his speech/utterance" (possessive), شاه شد is a simple past/result clause "the king became/was made," and در گمان is a prepositional phrase "into suspicion / in (the) belief" describing the state produced by those words.
segments.s160-g630: نکرد is the negative past of کردن "did not do / did not make." ایچ is the emphatic negative "nothing / not at all." بر کس پدید is an oblique phrase "appear before anyone / to any person" (بر = to/toward, کس = anyone), and از مهان specifies the class "of the guests/nobles." Together: "he did not at all make anything appear before anyone of the guests" = "he revealed nothing to any guest."
segments.s160-g631: بجایی (ب + جایی) functions adverbially as "in a proper/suitable place / in such a situation." که introduces the clause, کاری چنین = "a deed/act such as this," and اوفتاد is the past impersonal/archaic form "befell / occurred." The whole phrase means "in a case where such a thing happened."
segments.s160-g632: This is a nominal clause stating requirement: خرد باید = "wisdom is needed / wisdom is necessary," with the remaining nouns linked by و as an enumeration: "and knowledge and religion and justice." باید is the impersonal/modal "must / it is necessary," so the line lists qualities required in such a situation.
segments.s160-g617: This is a short past-tense clause in classical Persian with subject first (سیاوش) and the past verb ببوسید ('kissed') following it. تخت پدر is a genitive construction ('the throne of the father') without the modern ezafe; the whole phrase reads 'Siavash kissed (the) father's throne.'
segments.s160-g618: وزان is the compound 'و + زان' meaning 'and from that'; تخت is the noun governed by زان ('from that throne'). برخاست is the past 'rose/got up.' The conjunction و links a second verb آمد ('came') in the coordinated clause; بدر is here a proper name/destination mentioned without a preposition (classical Persian often omits به), so the clause means 'and (he) rose from that throne and came to Badr.'
segments.s160-g619: This is an adverbial/locative phrase describing who and where: شبستان 'the bedchamber' (i.e., those of the inner chamber), همه 'all/everyone,' پیش + سودابه = 'in the presence of Sudabeh' (پیش = 'before/in front of'), and باز here functions as an adverb 'again' (or 'once more'). The phrase sets the scene for the following verb.
segments.s160-g620: دویدند is the 3rd-person-plural past 'they ran/hurried.' The coordinated clause و بردند is 'and they brought/carried'; او را uses the object-marking را after the pronoun to mark the beneficiary/indirect object ('to her'), and نماز is the thing brought ('the prayer' — i.e., they brought prayer/one to pray). The word order places the pronoun + را before the noun being delivered, a common classical pattern.
segments.s160-g621: برین گونه is a fused adverbial 'بر این گونه' = 'in this manner/so'; بگذشت is the past 'passed' and takes یک روزگار ('a time/a day') as its logical subject. Word order here is flexible and poetic: the adverbial + verb precedes the temporal noun ('a time passed').
segments.s160-g622: This clause describes the resulting change of state. (برو) here functions as a connective/poetic particle meaning roughly 'thereupon' — its exact lexical senses in the provided list don't match the classical usage, so it is treated as a discourse particle. گرمتر is the comparative adjective 'hotter/more fervent'; شد is the past 'became'; دل شهریار is a genitive juxtaposition 'the king's heart' (no ezafe). Together: 'thereupon the king's heart became warmer.'
segments.s160-g623: چنان شد is a resultive phrase “became so” (چنان = so/thus; شد = became). دلش is the possessed noun (“his heart”), where ش is the enclitic possessive. باز here means “again.” از مهر اوی expresses cause/possession—“because of / from love (مهر) of her”; اوی is an archaic possessive/pronominal form referring back to the beloved.
segments.s160-g624: که introduces a result clause “so that/that.” دیده is the ocular subject “the eye(s).” نه برداشت is a negative past construction: برداشت (literally “lifting/removal”) here functions as the verbal idea “took away / turned away,” negated by نه = “did not / would not.” از چهر اوی is the source phrase “from her face,” with اوی again the archaic pronominal/possessive referring to her; together: “that his eyes did not remove themselves from her face.”
segments.s160-g601: چو is a subordinating conjunction meaning 'when' introducing a temporal clause. سودابه را is the definite object (را marking Sudabeh), and روی ('face') is the object that the verb concerns. برگاشتند is a past plural verb (literary) here meaning 'they turned up/raised/exposed' (literary برگاشتن/برگذاشتن); the verb agrees with a plural agent implied in the context.
segments.s160-g602: شبستان is the locus/subject 'the chamber/inner room'; همه ('all') modifies it ('the whole chamber' / 'everyone there'). بانگ برداشتند is an idiomatic verbal phrase 'to raise/utter a cry' with برداشتند in past plural; word order is subject + modifiers + object + verb (typical Persian).
segments.s160-g603: دل شاه کاووس is a possessive noun phrase 'the heart of King Kavus' (possessor follows the possessed noun in Persian). پردرد is a compound adjective (پر + درد) meaning 'full of pain/sorrow' and شد is the past-tense copula 'became'. The clause literally: 'the heart of King Kavus became sorrowful.'
segments.s160-g604: نهان داشت is a light-verb construction where نهان (here adverbially 'secretly/hidden') combines with داشت ('kept/held') to mean 'he kept (it) hidden' or 'he concealed it'. رنگ رخش is a possessive construction 'the color/complexion of his countenance' (رخش = his face/countenance), followed by زرد شد 'became pale/yellow' (زرد used poetically as 'pale').
segments.s160-g605: سیاوش is the subject; چنین is a manner adverb 'thus/so'. گفت is the past verb 'said' (note: the provided senses list gives noun senses for گفت, but here it functions as the verb 'said'), and با شهریار indicates the addressee 'to/with the king' (با + title used as 'to the king' in reported speech).
segments.s160-g606: که introduces the subordinate clause ('that/that...'); دل را is the direct object with را marking definiteness/emphasis. بدین کار is the prepositional phrase 'in/over this matter' (بدین = بـ + ین 'with/this'), where the segmentation in the word list is split (ب + دین) but functions as a single prepositional phrase. رنجه مدار is an idiomatic negative imperative: رنجه (verb stem 'be distressed/suffer') with مدار (poetic negative imperative form) gives 'do not distress' — so the full clause means 'do not distress the heart over this affair.'
segments.s160-g607: به من here marks the beneficiary/recipient 'for me' (or 'leave her to me'); بخش is used in the sense 'give/spare' or 'grant' (in classical register), سودابه را is the definite object 'Sudabeh', and زین گناه is 'from this sin' (زین = 'this' / 'from this' in older usage). The phrase requests that Sudabeh be spared/handed over to the speaker and shielded from punishment for the sin.
segments.s160-g608: پذیرد is a verb 'accept' (third-person form) used here in a potential/conditional sense. مگر works as 'unless'/'except that' introducing the condition: 'she will not (be saved) unless...' پند 'advice/admonition' and آید به راه 'she comes to the path' (آید = 'comes/turns', به راه = 'onto the right way/path'). Put together: 'She will not be saved/pardoned unless she accepts admonition and turns to the right path.'
